addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

<command-name>/model</command-name>
            <command-message>model</command-message>
            <command-args></command-args>
<local-command-stdout>Set model to [1mFable 5[22m and saved as your default for new sessions</local-command-stdout>

<local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

<command-name>/effort</command-name>
            <command-message>effort</command-message>
            <command-args></command-args>
<local-command-stdout>Set effort level to high (saved as your default for new sessions): Comprehensive implementation with extensive testing and documentation</local-command-stdout>

I've been thinking and there are two ways to use tprobe, one is in
a benchmark where trpobe is a fundamental part of the application.
But I think, if successful, the majority of the use cases is going
to be instrumentation in applications which are not benchmarks.
Instead it will be used to discover the critical paths and to
monitor the app generally. To that end there are three distinct
phases:
- data collection
- analysis
- presentation.

Our current direction is to do the analysis at the point of data
collection but that means cycles will be used on critical/hot paths
but not used until later. Therefore I think it would be better
to save the data separate from analysis and presentation. This is
especially critical on an embedded system where the data will
definitely not be directly presented on the device and thus shouldn't
be analyzed as collected.

Therefore I've concluded we should buffer the data and periodically
send it to other threads/process/machines which will do the analysis
and presentation.

Thoughts?

addedBlocks:
## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

aiTitle: Separate data collection from analysis and presentation

1	# tprobe — founding rationale & consolidation plan
2	
3	This crate exists to end a four-way copy-paste. A tick-counter
4	measurement probe was vendored independently into four repos
5	(actor-x1, zc-ring-x1, iiac-perf, and prospectively zc-msg-x1)
6	and drifted in each. This note is the founding record: why we
7	are consolidating those copies into one crate, no_std-first, and
8	what that crate should be. It is the writeup of the design
9	discussion that created this repo.
10	
11	## The problem: four divergent copies, none no_std
12	
13	Three independent copies of the tick-probe exist, drifted apart —
14	not just vendored duplicates but three different scope decisions:
15	
16	- **iiac-perf** (`src/tprobe.rs` + `src/tprobe2.rs` +
17	  `src/band_table.rs`, ~442 lines total, in the 0.20.0 binary) —
18	  a minimal probe core with the full calibrated harness built
19	  around it in the same crate and is the original version.
20	- **actor-x1** (`crates/tprobe`, v0.1.5) — the richest:
21	  `tprobe.rs` (~312 lines) folds `overhead.rs`
22	  (apparatus-overhead calibration) and `pin.rs` (CPU pinning)
23	  _into_ the probe crate. And is based on iiac-perf version.
24	- **zc-ring-x1** (`tprobe` v0.1.0 + sibling `tp_runner`) — split
25	  the other way: a bare `tprobe` crate (probe + `ticks` +
26	  `band_table` + `tprobe_span`) and a separate `tp_runner` crate
27	  holding the drive loop, pinning, and Linux `perf_event_open`
28	  counters. `tprobe.rs` is ~110 lines.
29	- **zc-msg-x1** — would have been a forth copy if its seeding
30	  plan (path-dep zc-ring-x1's `tprobe`/`tp_runner`) were followed
31	  as written. Consolidating here is what stops that.
32	
33	Two facts fall out of comparing them:
34	
35	- **The duplication is real and drifting.** Three live,
36	  independently-edited forks — one bundling pin+overhead, one
37	  splitting them out, one minimal — is the DRY trigger the
38	  earlier "extract when a second consumer materializes" note was
39	  waiting for. We think each fork happened because there was no
40	  shared crate to depend on at the time, so each repo vendored
41	  and then edited locally.
42	- **None of the copies is `no_std`.** Every one leans on
43	  `hdrhistogram` (std), `String` probe names, and std time /
44	  `minstant`. So today nothing here can measure on an embedded
45	  target — the copies are all host-only.
46	
47	## Scope: a probe is not a harness
48	
49	The existing code already draws a clean line, worth preserving:
50	
51	- **Probe** (this crate) — read a hardware tick counter, record
52	  deltas into a named histogram, render a percentile band-table.
53	  Small, embeddable, ideally `no_std`.
54	- **Harness** (iiac-perf; zc-ring's `tp_runner`) — adaptive loop
55	  sizing, apparatus-overhead subtraction, thread pinning, a bench
56	  registry, `perf_event_open` counters. zc-ring's `tp_runner`
57	  doc says it plainly: "Deliberately not a benchmark harness …
58	  for that scale of machinery use iiac-perf."
59	
60	The consolidation target is the **probe**. The harness layer
61	(sizing, calibration, registry) stays where it is — iiac-perf
62	owns host-side characterization and already produces the
63	published percentile tables.
64	
65	## Why not an off-the-shelf benchmarking crate
66	
67	The perf-book benchmarking page [[1]] lists criterion, divan,
68	hyperfine, iai/gungraun (callgrind), bencher, and rustc-perf.
69	None replaces the probe for the need that motivates it — a
70	nanosecond-scale, cross-core, tail-latency _distribution_ of a
71	lock-free hot path, measurable on the target:
72	
73	- **criterion / divan** — host-std statistical microbench
74	  harnesses. Good for A/B + throughput at the _mean_; they do not
75	  surface deep tail bands (the n5..n10 / p.999_999_999 rows these
76	  measurements are about — batch-timing discards the
77	  distribution). Both are `std`, so neither runs on an embedded
78	  target. divan is additionally stale (last release April 2025).
79	- **iai / gungraun (callgrind)** — instruction-count / cache
80	  _simulation_. Noise-free and great for CI regression guards,
81	  but they model a single simulated run and cannot reproduce the
82	  real cross-core cache-line-transfer economics that is the whole
83	  point (the 26–40% SPSC-vs-MPSC gap zc-ring measured is a
84	  hardware effect) [[2]].
85	- **hyperfine** — whole-process wall-clock timing; wrong
86	  granularity for ns round-trips. **bencher** — a CI tracking
87	  layer, orthogonal to how the numbers are produced.
88	
89	So for _host-side_ CI regression gating an off-the-shelf harness
90	would work, but iiac-perf already covers host-side, making a
91	second one largely redundant; and for _on-target_ embedded
92	measurement nothing off-the-shelf applies at all. This
93	home-grown probe is the only thing that can measure on the
94	target — which is why it is worth investing in rather than
95	replacing.
96	
97	## The design: no_std core + std feature layer
98	
99	This crate should not be a file-move of any one fork (that just
100	relocates std code and freezes one of three designs). It should
101	be a redesign toward a `no_std` core with an opt-in `std` layer:
102	
103	- **`no_std`, no-alloc histogram** — a fixed log-linear bucket
104	  histogram replacing `hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for
105	  embedded). This is the real design nut. We think a small
106	  fixed-bucket table sized like the existing band scheme is
107	  enough for the tail bands we report; evaluate whether any
108	  existing `no_std` histogram crate fits before hand-rolling.
109	- **`&'static str` names** instead of `String`, so the core
110	  needs no allocator.
111	- **Cycle-counter abstraction** — the per-arch `ticks/`
112	  (`x86_64` `rdtsc`, `aarch64` `CNTVCT_EL0`) is already close to
113	  `no_std`; put it behind a portable-atomic-style trait so
114	  CAS-less / counter-less targets can supply their own source.
115	- **`std` feature** — the rich band-table reporting
116	  (thousands-separator formatting, tick→ns calibration display),
117	  and optionally the pinning / `perf_event_open` counters, gated
118	  so a mainstream host build keeps everything and an embedded
119	  build gets just the core.
120	- **Spans** — the span-style API exists in some copies
121	  (`tprobe_span.rs` in zc-ring, `tprobe2.rs` in iiac-perf) and
122	  not in actor-x1; fold in as an optional part of the core, same
123	  `no_std` discipline.
124	
125	## Sequencing: this crate before zc-msg-x1's messaging layer
126	
127	Chosen path (decision "B" in the originating discussion):
128	consolidate `tprobe` here **before** seeding zc-msg-x1's
129	messaging layer. Seeding zc-msg on a std copy we intend to
130	replace is throwaway work, and measurement is first-class enough
131	across this repo family (embedded/on-target is a stated
132	requirement, not a nice-to-have) to justify fixing the
133	foundation first. The rejected alternative was to path-dep
134	zc-ring's existing std `tprobe` into zc-msg now and defer no_std
135	— faster to start, but it adds a fifth copy and discards work
136	later.
137	
138	## Consumers and migration
139	
140	This crate should let the live consumers converge; migration is
141	per-repo and not all at once:
142	
143	- **actor-x1** — active consumer and the probe's birthplace; a
144	  prime candidate to drop its vendored copy for this crate.
145	- **iiac-perf** — active; today it _forks_ rather than depends
146	  (uses `minstant` and its own band code). Confirm whether that
147	  fork is deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental before
148	  assuming it will depend on this crate.
149	- **zc-ring-x1** — frozen lab notebook; we think we will **not**
150	  migrate it (leave its `tprobe`/`tp_runner` as the record).
151	- **zc-msg-x1** — the new consumer; starts on this crate instead
152	  of a vendored copy.
153	
154	## Name
155	
156	Kept **`tprobe`** — already the crate name in all four repos
157	(recognition carries over), short, and "t" reads as tick/time,
158	what it counts. The only more-descriptive alternative considered
159	was `tickprobe`; the added clarity did not seem worth losing the
160	continuity.
161	
162	## Open questions
163	
164	- Which `no_std` histogram: an existing crate or a hand-rolled
165	  fixed-bucket table? Drives the core's shape.
166	- Is iiac-perf's fork deliberate? Decides whether consolidation
167	  is three-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg + iiac-perf depend) or two.
168	- Where do pinning and `perf_event_open` live — in the `std`
169	  feature of this crate, or kept in a separate runner/harness
170	  crate as zc-ring split them?
171	
172	# References
173	
174	[1]: https://nnethercote.github.io/perf-book/benchmarking.html
175	[2]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1
176

# tprobe — founding rationale & consolidation plan

This crate exists to end a four-way copy-paste. A tick-counter
measurement probe was vendored independently into four repos
(actor-x1, zc-ring-x1, iiac-perf, and prospectively zc-msg-x1)
and drifted in each. This note is the founding record: why we
are consolidating those copies into one crate, no_std-first, and
what that crate should be. It is the writeup of the design
discussion that created this repo.

## The problem: four divergent copies, none no_std

Three independent copies of the tick-probe exist, drifted apart —
not just vendored duplicates but three different scope decisions:

- **iiac-perf** (`src/tprobe.rs` + `src/tprobe2.rs` +
  `src/band_table.rs`, ~442 lines total, in the 0.20.0 binary) —
  a minimal probe core with the full calibrated harness built
  around it in the same crate and is the original version.
- **actor-x1** (`crates/tprobe`, v0.1.5) — the richest:
  `tprobe.rs` (~312 lines) folds `overhead.rs`
  (apparatus-overhead calibration) and `pin.rs` (CPU pinning)
  _into_ the probe crate. And is based on iiac-perf version.
- **zc-ring-x1** (`tprobe` v0.1.0 + sibling `tp_runner`) — split
  the other way: a bare `tprobe` crate (probe + `ticks` +
  `band_table` + `tprobe_span`) and a separate `tp_runner` crate
  holding the drive loop, pinning, and Linux `perf_event_open`
  counters. `tprobe.rs` is ~110 lines.
- **zc-msg-x1** — would have been a forth copy if its seeding
  plan (path-dep zc-ring-x1's `tprobe`/`tp_runner`) were followed
  as written. Consolidating here is what stops that.

Two facts fall out of comparing them:

- **The duplication is real and drifting.** Three live,
  independently-edited forks — one bundling pin+overhead, one
  splitting them out, one minimal — is the DRY trigger the
  earlier "extract when a second consumer materializes" note was
  waiting for. We think each fork happened because there was no
  shared crate to depend on at the time, so each repo vendored
  and then edited locally.
- **None of the copies is `no_std`.** Every one leans on
  `hdrhistogram` (std), `String` probe names, and std time /
  `minstant`. So today nothing here can measure on an embedded
  target — the copies are all host-only.

## Scope: a probe is not a harness

The existing code already draws a clean line, worth preserving:

- **Probe** (this crate) — read a hardware tick counter, record
  deltas into a named histogram, render a percentile band-table.
  Small, embeddable, ideally `no_std`.
- **Harness** (iiac-perf; zc-ring's `tp_runner`) — adaptive loop
  sizing, apparatus-overhead subtraction, thread pinning, a bench
  registry, `perf_event_open` counters. zc-ring's `tp_runner`
  doc says it plainly: "Deliberately not a benchmark harness …
  for that scale of machinery use iiac-perf."

The consolidation target is the **probe**. The harness layer
(sizing, calibration, registry) stays where it is — iiac-perf
owns host-side characterization and already produces the
published percentile tables.

## Why not an off-the-shelf benchmarking crate

The perf-book benchmarking page [[1]] lists criterion, divan,
hyperfine, iai/gungraun (callgrind), bencher, and rustc-perf.
None replaces the probe for the need that motivates it — a
nanosecond-scale, cross-core, tail-latency _distribution_ of a
lock-free hot path, measurable on the target:

- **criterion / divan** — host-std statistical microbench
  harnesses. Good for A/B + throughput at the _mean_; they do not
  surface deep tail bands (the n5..n10 / p.999_999_999 rows these
  measurements are about — batch-timing discards the
  distribution). Both are `std`, so neither runs on an embedded
  target. divan is additionally stale (last release April 2025).
- **iai / gungraun (callgrind)** — instruction-count / cache
  _simulation_. Noise-free and great for CI regression guards,
  but they model a single simulated run and cannot reproduce the
  real cross-core cache-line-transfer economics that is the whole
  point (the 26–40% SPSC-vs-MPSC gap zc-ring measured is a
  hardware effect) [[2]].
- **hyperfine** — whole-process wall-clock timing; wrong
  granularity for ns round-trips. **bencher** — a CI tracking
  layer, orthogonal to how the numbers are produced.

So for _host-side_ CI regression gating an off-the-shelf harness
would work, but iiac-perf already covers host-side, making a
second one largely redundant; and for _on-target_ embedded
measurement nothing off-the-shelf applies at all. This
home-grown probe is the only thing that can measure on the
target — which is why it is worth investing in rather than
replacing.

## The design: no_std core + std feature layer

This crate should not be a file-move of any one fork (that just
relocates std code and freezes one of three designs). It should
be a redesign toward a `no_std` core with an opt-in `std` layer:

- **`no_std`, no-alloc histogram** — a fixed log-linear bucket
  histogram replacing `hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for
  embedded). This is the real design nut. We think a small
  fixed-bucket table sized like the existing band scheme is
  enough for the tail bands we report; evaluate whether any
  existing `no_std` histogram crate fits before hand-rolling.
- **`&'static str` names** instead of `String`, so the core
  needs no allocator.
- **Cycle-counter abstraction** — the per-arch `ticks/`
  (`x86_64` `rdtsc`, `aarch64` `CNTVCT_EL0`) is already close to
  `no_std`; put it behind a portable-atomic-style trait so
  CAS-less / counter-less targets can supply their own source.
- **`std` feature** — the rich band-table reporting
  (thousands-separator formatting, tick→ns calibration display),
  and optionally the pinning / `perf_event_open` counters, gated
  so a mainstream host build keeps everything and an embedded
  build gets just the core.
- **Spans** — the span-style API exists in some copies
  (`tprobe_span.rs` in zc-ring, `tprobe2.rs` in iiac-perf) and
  not in actor-x1; fold in as an optional part of the core, same
  `no_std` discipline.

## Sequencing: this crate before zc-msg-x1's messaging layer

Chosen path (decision "B" in the originating discussion):
consolidate `tprobe` here **before** seeding zc-msg-x1's
messaging layer. Seeding zc-msg on a std copy we intend to
replace is throwaway work, and measurement is first-class enough
across this repo family (embedded/on-target is a stated
requirement, not a nice-to-have) to justify fixing the
foundation first. The rejected alternative was to path-dep
zc-ring's existing std `tprobe` into zc-msg now and defer no_std
— faster to start, but it adds a fifth copy and discards work
later.

## Consumers and migration

This crate should let the live consumers converge; migration is
per-repo and not all at once:

- **actor-x1** — active consumer and the probe's birthplace; a
  prime candidate to drop its vendored copy for this crate.
- **iiac-perf** — active; today it _forks_ rather than depends
  (uses `minstant` and its own band code). Confirm whether that
  fork is deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental before
  assuming it will depend on this crate.
- **zc-ring-x1** — frozen lab notebook; we think we will **not**
  migrate it (leave its `tprobe`/`tp_runner` as the record).
- **zc-msg-x1** — the new consumer; starts on this crate instead
  of a vendored copy.

## Name

Kept **`tprobe`** — already the crate name in all four repos
(recognition carries over), short, and "t" reads as tick/time,
what it counts. The only more-descriptive alternative considered
was `tickprobe`; the added clarity did not seem worth losing the
continuity.

## Open questions

- Which `no_std` histogram: an existing crate or a hand-rolled
  fixed-bucket table? Drives the core's shape.
- Is iiac-perf's fork deliberate? Decides whether consolidation
  is three-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg + iiac-perf depend) or two.
- Where do pinning and `perf_event_open` live — in the `std`
  feature of this crate, or kept in a separate runner/harness
  crate as zc-ring split them?

# References

[1]: https://nnethercote.github.io/perf-book/benchmarking.html
[2]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1

lastPrompt: I've been thinking and there are two ways to use tprobe, one is in a benchmark where trpobe is a fundamental part of the application. But I think, if successful, the majority of the use cases is going…

aiTitle: Separate data collection from analysis and presentation

content.text:
I think the instinct — separate collection from analysis/presentation — is right, and it's worth pinning down in design.md. But there's one framing refinement I'd push on: **histogramming isn't analysis, it's compression**, and that changes what "buffer the data" should mean.

Three options for what the hot path writes:

1. **Raw samples into a ring buffer**, shipped off wholesale. Purest separation, retains everything (including time-ordering). But the data volume is unbounded by design: 8 bytes/event at, say, 10M events/sec on a hot path is 80 MB/s. On an embedded target with a UART or a slow link, that's exactly the device that *can't* ship it. The bandwidth cost also isn't free on the hot path itself — streaming writes through memory evict cache the measured code wanted.

2. **Histogram at collection, render at collection** — the current copies. Analysis deferred, presentation not; and the presentation code (formatting, band tables) drags in std.

3. **Histogram at collection, snapshot-and-ship** — the hot path does only the log-linear bucket increment (a leading-zeros + shift + counter bump, single-digit ns, touches a small cache-resident table). Periodically the histogram is snapshotted (atomic swap / double-buffer so the hot path never blocks) and the snapshot — a fixed few-hundred-bytes-to-few-KB blob regardless of event rate — is shipped to another thread/process/machine. *All* percentile extraction, band tables, and formatting happen there.

I'd recommend **3 as the default architecture, with 1 as an optional feature-gated mode**. Reasoning:

- The distribution is what your use cases (tail bands, p.999…) actually consume, and the histogram is a lossy-only-in-bucket-resolution compression of it. Deferring the bucket increment buys you almost nothing (it's a few ns) while costing you 8 bytes/event of bandwidth. This is the shape production telemetry converged on (HdrHistogram interval logs, Prometheus counters) for the same reason.
- Raw samples do carry something histograms discard: **time ordering**. "Latency spiked when X happened," periodic patterns, first-N-events-after-startup. That's a flight-recorder/trace use case — real, but distinct — and fits as a `raw` mode (last-N ring, or sampled 1-in-K) rather than the default.
- For embedded, 3 is the only one of the three that works at all rates: fixed-size periodic snapshots fit any link.

Design consequences if you agree:

- The core's contract becomes: hot path records; **snapshot/drain is a first-class operation** (wait-free for the recorder — double-buffered counters or an epoch swap).
- The snapshot needs a small **versioned, no_std wire format** (bucket layout params + counts). That's the boundary between the crate and the world.
- Presentation (band-table rendering) operates *only* on snapshots and moves cleanly into the std layer — or even a separate reader crate, which also answers the open question about where pinning/perf_event live: with the reader/harness, not the probe.
- Transport itself (which thread, which socket, which UART) stays **out of the probe crate** — the probe produces snapshots, the app moves them. Worth noting the synergy though: shipping snapshots between threads is literally what zc-ring/zc-msg do, so tprobe stays dependency-free while the family composes.

One honest caveat, speculation-marked per house style: we think the bucket-increment cost is negligible on the hot path, but that's exactly the kind of claim tprobe itself should measure — probe-overhead calibration (which actor-x1's `overhead.rs` already does) should compare histogram-increment vs. raw-ring-write as one of the first experiments.

Want me to fold this into design.md as a new section (collection/analysis/presentation phases + the snapshot-and-ship decision), including updating the open questions?

message.content:
But if we ship the data to a separate lower priority thread for compression that would seem worth it,
in a benchmark scenario where the "work" is the measurement, there may not be any cycles available for
for a lower priority thread. In iiac-perf I wonder how it might work if instead of timing the outer and
inner, if instead we summed the inner loops to compute the duration, that would allow the tprobe
analysis to be done for free, i.e. off the measured path.

Thoughts?

content.text:
Both points land, and together they actually sharpen the architecture rather than complicate it. Let me take them in turn.

**Raw-ship to a collector thread in apps: yes, make it a first-class mode.** The refinement from last round survives, though: the histogram doesn't go away, it just *moves*. The collector thread receiving raw deltas builds the same histogram the hot path would have built in-place — so the compression point is a deployment decision, not two different systems:

- **Within one machine** (instrumented app, spare cores): raw ring → collector thread. 10M events/s × 8B = 80 MB/s, trivial against tens-of-GB/s memory bandwidth. Cheapest possible hot-path op, and this is literally zc-ring's domain — an SPSC ring per instrumented thread.
- **Off-machine / embedded link**: compress (histogram) on device, ship fixed-size snapshots. Forced by link bandwidth, as before.

Design consequence: the core probe becomes tick-read + delta + a pluggable **Recorder** trait, with `RingRecorder` (raw ship) and `HistogramRecorder` (in-place compress) as sibling no_std impls. Downstream analysis/presentation consumes histograms either way; the wire-format boundary stays.

**The benchmark scenario: I'd go one step further than "lower-priority thread."** A concurrent thread doing compression *during* measurement is exactly what a benchmark doesn't want — it competes for L3, memory bandwidth, possibly an SMT sibling, and perturbs the thing being measured. But benchmarks have two properties apps lack: a **bounded iteration count** and a natural **"after."** So the bench-mode recorder is even simpler:

- **`ArrayRecorder`**: preallocated `Vec<u32>`/`[u32; N]` of deltas, one sequential store per iteration (store-buffer absorbs it), no thread at all. Analyze after the loop completes — truly free, in the sense that zero analysis cycles overlap any measurement.

**On summing the inner deltas for duration:** yes, and there's a bonus hiding in it. Note that `sum(inner deltas) ≠ outer duration` — the gap is the per-iteration harness cost (tick reads, the record store, loop overhead). That inequality is a feature:

- `sum(inner)` = time actually spent in the measured op — arguably the *right* numerator for op throughput.
- `outer − sum(inner)` = directly **measured** apparatus overhead for that exact run — today iiac-perf/actor-x1 *estimate* this via a separate calibration pass (`overhead.rs`); this gives it per-run, for free, as a self-check that calibration still matches reality.

So I'd keep timing the outer loop, but demote it from "the measurement" to "the cross-check."

One speculation-marked caveat: we think the raw store is cheaper on the hot path than the histogram increment, but the histogram's table is L1-resident while the raw array streams through cache — for very long runs the eviction pressure could invert that. It's the first experiment the consolidated tprobe should run against itself.

Net design.md impact: the three phases become collection (probe + pluggable Recorder: array / ring / histogram), analysis (histogram build + percentiles, always off the measured path — after the loop, on a collector, or off-device), presentation (std-only, operates on snapshots). Want me to write that in?

message.content: Yes, write it into design.md

input.old_string: ## Sequencing: this crate before zc-msg-x1's messaging layer
input.new_string:
## Phases: collection, analysis, presentation

Two use classes shape the crate. In a **benchmark** the
measurement is the work (iiac-perf, the actor-x1 benches). In an
**instrumented application** the probe discovers critical paths
and monitors the app generally — we think this is the majority
use case if the crate succeeds, including embedded targets where
data is never presented on the device. The existing copies fold
analysis into collection (histogram update on the hot path,
rendering in-process); the consolidated design separates three
phases and keeps everything but raw collection off the measured
path:

- **Collection** — the hot path does a tick read, a delta, and
  one recorder operation; nothing else. The recorder is a
  pluggable trait with three `no_std` impls:
  - `ArrayRecorder` — preallocated delta array, one sequential
    store per event. For benchmarks: bounded iteration counts
    and a natural "after" mean no concurrent thread is needed;
    analysis runs after the loop with zero overlap with any
    measurement.
  - `RingRecorder` — raw deltas into an SPSC ring drained by a
    collector thread. For instrumented apps with spare cores:
    at 10M events/s the raw stream is ~80 MB/s, trivial against
    within-machine memory bandwidth. Shipping buffers between
    threads is zc-ring's domain — a natural composition, though
    transport stays out of this crate.
  - `HistogramRecorder` — in-place log-linear bucket increment.
    For embedded / off-machine links where raw-sample bandwidth
    is infeasible; the device ships fixed-size snapshots
    instead.
- **Analysis** — histogram build and percentile extraction.
  Always off the measured path: after the loop (array), on the
  collector thread (ring), or off-device (histogram snapshots).
- **Presentation** — band-table rendering, formatting, tick→ns
  display. `std`-only, operates on snapshots, never on the
  measuring device.

The organizing insight: **histogramming is compression, not
analysis**. A log-linear histogram compresses the delta
distribution, lossy only in bucket resolution, and it is the
interchange format between phases wherever compression happens.
The recorder choice just moves the compression point — after the
run, on a collector core, or on the device — so analysis and
presentation downstream are identical in all three modes.

Consequences for the core:

- **Snapshot/drain is a first-class operation** — wait-free for
  the recorder (double-buffered counts or an epoch swap), so a
  collector can harvest without blocking the hot path.
- **A small versioned `no_std` wire format** for histogram
  snapshots (bucket-layout parameters + counts) is the boundary
  between this crate and the world.
- **Transport is out of scope** — the probe produces snapshots
  and raw rings; which thread, socket, or UART moves them is
  the application's business.
- Raw recorders retain **time ordering** that histograms
  discard (correlating spikes with events, periodic patterns) —
  a flight-recorder use distinct from distribution measurement,
  and a reason the raw modes are first-class rather than merely
  an optimization.

### Outer loop as cross-check, not measurement

For the benchmark harness (iiac-perf), summing the recorded
inner deltas gives the duration directly, which demotes the
outer-loop timing from "the measurement" to a cross-check worth
keeping:

- `sum(inner deltas)` — time actually spent in the measured op;
  the right numerator for op throughput.
- `outer − sum(inner)` — the per-iteration apparatus cost (tick
  reads, the record store, loop overhead), *measured* for that
  exact run. Today the copies estimate this with a separate
  calibration pass (actor-x1's `overhead.rs`); this makes it a
  free per-run self-check that calibration matches reality.

### First experiment: recorder cost

We think the raw array/ring store is cheaper per event than the
histogram increment (a store vs. leading-zeros + shift + counter
bump), but the histogram's table stays L1-resident while a raw
array streams through cache — for long runs the eviction
pressure could invert the ranking. Comparing recorder impls is
the first experiment the consolidated tprobe should run against
itself, folded into probe-overhead calibration.

## Sequencing: this crate before zc-msg-x1's messaging layer

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# tprobe — founding rationale & consolidation plan

This crate exists to end a four-way copy-paste. A tick-counter
measurement probe was vendored independently into four repos
(actor-x1, zc-ring-x1, iiac-perf, and prospectively zc-msg-x1)
and drifted in each. This note is the founding record: why we
are consolidating those copies into one crate, no_std-first, and
what that crate should be. It is the writeup of the design
discussion that created this repo.

## The problem: four divergent copies, none no_std

Three independent copies of the tick-probe exist, drifted apart —
not just vendored duplicates but three different scope decisions:

- **iiac-perf** (`src/tprobe.rs` + `src/tprobe2.rs` +
  `src/band_table.rs`, ~442 lines total, in the 0.20.0 binary) —
  a minimal probe core with the full calibrated harness built
  around it in the same crate and is the original version.
- **actor-x1** (`crates/tprobe`, v0.1.5) — the richest:
  `tprobe.rs` (~312 lines) folds `overhead.rs`
  (apparatus-overhead calibration) and `pin.rs` (CPU pinning)
  _into_ the probe crate. And is based on iiac-perf version.
- **zc-ring-x1** (`tprobe` v0.1.0 + sibling `tp_runner`) — split
  the other way: a bare `tprobe` crate (probe + `ticks` +
  `band_table` + `tprobe_span`) and a separate `tp_runner` crate
  holding the drive loop, pinning, and Linux `perf_event_open`
  counters. `tprobe.rs` is ~110 lines.
- **zc-msg-x1** — would have been a forth copy if its seeding
  plan (path-dep zc-ring-x1's `tprobe`/`tp_runner`) were followed
  as written. Consolidating here is what stops that.

Two facts fall out of comparing them:

- **The duplication is real and drifting.** Three live,
  independently-edited forks — one bundling pin+overhead, one
  splitting them out, one minimal — is the DRY trigger the
  earlier "extract when a second consumer materializes" note was
  waiting for. We think each fork happened because there was no
  shared crate to depend on at the time, so each repo vendored
  and then edited locally.
- **None of the copies is `no_std`.** Every one leans on
  `hdrhistogram` (std), `String` probe names, and std time /
  `minstant`. So today nothing here can measure on an embedded
  target — the copies are all host-only.

## Scope: a probe is not a harness

The existing code already draws a clean line, worth preserving:

- **Probe** (this crate) — read a hardware tick counter, record
  deltas into a named histogram, render a percentile band-table.
  Small, embeddable, ideally `no_std`.
- **Harness** (iiac-perf; zc-ring's `tp_runner`) — adaptive loop
  sizing, apparatus-overhead subtraction, thread pinning, a bench
  registry, `perf_event_open` counters. zc-ring's `tp_runner`
  doc says it plainly: "Deliberately not a benchmark harness …
  for that scale of machinery use iiac-perf."

The consolidation target is the **probe**. The harness layer
(sizing, calibration, registry) stays where it is — iiac-perf
owns host-side characterization and already produces the
published percentile tables.

## Why not an off-the-shelf benchmarking crate

The perf-book benchmarking page [[1]] lists criterion, divan,
hyperfine, iai/gungraun (callgrind), bencher, and rustc-perf.
None replaces the probe for the need that motivates it — a
nanosecond-scale, cross-core, tail-latency _distribution_ of a
lock-free hot path, measurable on the target:

- **criterion / divan** — host-std statistical microbench
  harnesses. Good for A/B + throughput at the _mean_; they do not
  surface deep tail bands (the n5..n10 / p.999_999_999 rows these
  measurements are about — batch-timing discards the
  distribution). Both are `std`, so neither runs on an embedded
  target. divan is additionally stale (last release April 2025).
- **iai / gungraun (callgrind)** — instruction-count / cache
  _simulation_. Noise-free and great for CI regression guards,
  but they model a single simulated run and cannot reproduce the
  real cross-core cache-line-transfer economics that is the whole
  point (the 26–40% SPSC-vs-MPSC gap zc-ring measured is a
  hardware effect) [[2]].
- **hyperfine** — whole-process wall-clock timing; wrong
  granularity for ns round-trips. **bencher** — a CI tracking
  layer, orthogonal to how the numbers are produced.

So for _host-side_ CI regression gating an off-the-shelf harness
would work, but iiac-perf already covers host-side, making a
second one largely redundant; and for _on-target_ embedded
measurement nothing off-the-shelf applies at all. This
home-grown probe is the only thing that can measure on the
target — which is why it is worth investing in rather than
replacing.

## The design: no_std core + std feature layer

This crate should not be a file-move of any one fork (that just
relocates std code and freezes one of three designs). It should
be a redesign toward a `no_std` core with an opt-in `std` layer:

- **`no_std`, no-alloc histogram** — a fixed log-linear bucket
  histogram replacing `hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for
  embedded). This is the real design nut. We think a small
  fixed-bucket table sized like the existing band scheme is
  enough for the tail bands we report; evaluate whether any
  existing `no_std` histogram crate fits before hand-rolling.
- **`&'static str` names** instead of `String`, so the core
  needs no allocator.
- **Cycle-counter abstraction** — the per-arch `ticks/`
  (`x86_64` `rdtsc`, `aarch64` `CNTVCT_EL0`) is already close to
  `no_std`; put it behind a portable-atomic-style trait so
  CAS-less / counter-less targets can supply their own source.
- **`std` feature** — the rich band-table reporting
  (thousands-separator formatting, tick→ns calibration display),
  and optionally the pinning / `perf_event_open` counters, gated
  so a mainstream host build keeps everything and an embedded
  build gets just the core.
- **Spans** — the span-style API exists in some copies
  (`tprobe_span.rs` in zc-ring, `tprobe2.rs` in iiac-perf) and
  not in actor-x1; fold in as an optional part of the core, same
  `no_std` discipline.

## Sequencing: this crate before zc-msg-x1's messaging layer

Chosen path (decision "B" in the originating discussion):
consolidate `tprobe` here **before** seeding zc-msg-x1's
messaging layer. Seeding zc-msg on a std copy we intend to
replace is throwaway work, and measurement is first-class enough
across this repo family (embedded/on-target is a stated
requirement, not a nice-to-have) to justify fixing the
foundation first. The rejected alternative was to path-dep
zc-ring's existing std `tprobe` into zc-msg now and defer no_std
— faster to start, but it adds a fifth copy and discards work
later.

## Consumers and migration

This crate should let the live consumers converge; migration is
per-repo and not all at once:

- **actor-x1** — active consumer and the probe's birthplace; a
  prime candidate to drop its vendored copy for this crate.
- **iiac-perf** — active; today it _forks_ rather than depends
  (uses `minstant` and its own band code). Confirm whether that
  fork is deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental before
  assuming it will depend on this crate.
- **zc-ring-x1** — frozen lab notebook; we think we will **not**
  migrate it (leave its `tprobe`/`tp_runner` as the record).
- **zc-msg-x1** — the new consumer; starts on this crate instead
  of a vendored copy.

## Name

Kept **`tprobe`** — already the crate name in all four repos
(recognition carries over), short, and "t" reads as tick/time,
what it counts. The only more-descriptive alternative considered
was `tickprobe`; the added clarity did not seem worth losing the
continuity.

## Open questions

- Which `no_std` histogram: an existing crate or a hand-rolled
  fixed-bucket table? Drives the core's shape.
- Is iiac-perf's fork deliberate? Decides whether consolidation
  is three-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg + iiac-perf depend) or two.
- Where do pinning and `perf_event_open` live — in the `std`
  feature of this crate, or kept in a separate runner/harness
  crate as zc-ring split them?

# References

[1]: https://nnethercote.github.io/perf-book/benchmarking.html
[2]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1

+- **Transport is out of scope** — the probe produces snapshots
+  and raw rings; which thread, socket, or UART moves them is
+  the application's business.
+- Raw recorders retain **time ordering** that histograms
+  discard (correlating spikes with events, periodic patterns) —
+  a flight-recorder use distinct from distribution measurement,
+  and a reason the raw modes are first-class rather than merely
+  an optimization.
+### Outer loop as cross-check, not measurement
+For the benchmark harness (iiac-perf), summing the recorded
+inner deltas gives the duration directly, which demotes the
+outer-loop timing from "the measurement" to a cross-check worth
+keeping:
+- `sum(inner deltas)` — time actually spent in the measured op;
+  the right numerator for op throughput.
+- `outer − sum(inner)` — the per-iteration apparatus cost (tick
+  reads, the record store, loop overhead), *measured* for that
+  exact run. Today the copies estimate this with a separate
+  calibration pass (actor-x1's `overhead.rs`); this makes it a
+  free per-run self-check that calibration matches reality.
+### First experiment: recorder cost
+We think the raw array/ring store is cheaper per event than the
+histogram increment (a store vs. leading-zeros + shift + counter
+bump), but the histogram's table stays L1-resident while a raw
+array streams through cache — for long runs the eviction
+pressure could invert the ranking. Comparing recorder impls is
+the first experiment the consolidated tprobe should run against
+itself, folded into probe-overhead calibration.

Chosen path (decision "B" in the originating discussion):

1	# tprobe — founding rationale & consolidation plan
2	
3	This crate exists to end a four-way copy-paste. A tick-counter
4	measurement probe was vendored independently into four repos
5	(actor-x1, zc-ring-x1, iiac-perf, and prospectively zc-msg-x1)
6	and drifted in each. This note is the founding record: why we
7	are consolidating those copies into one crate, no_std-first, and
8	what that crate should be. It is the writeup of the design
9	discussion that created this repo.
10	
11	## The problem: four divergent copies, none no_std
12	
13	Three independent copies of the tick-probe exist, drifted apart —
14	not just vendored duplicates but three different scope decisions:
15	
16	- **iiac-perf** (`src/tprobe.rs` + `src/tprobe2.rs` +
17	  `src/band_table.rs`, ~442 lines total, in the 0.20.0 binary) —
18	  a minimal probe core with the full calibrated harness built
19	  around it in the same crate and is the original version.
20	- **actor-x1** (`crates/tprobe`, v0.1.5) — the richest:
21	  `tprobe.rs` (~312 lines) folds `overhead.rs`
22	  (apparatus-overhead calibration) and `pin.rs` (CPU pinning)
23	  _into_ the probe crate. And is based on iiac-perf version.
24	- **zc-ring-x1** (`tprobe` v0.1.0 + sibling `tp_runner`) — split
25	  the other way: a bare `tprobe` crate (probe + `ticks` +
26	  `band_table` + `tprobe_span`) and a separate `tp_runner` crate
27	  holding the drive loop, pinning, and Linux `perf_event_open`
28	  counters. `tprobe.rs` is ~110 lines.
29	- **zc-msg-x1** — would have been a forth copy if its seeding
30	  plan (path-dep zc-ring-x1's `tprobe`/`tp_runner`) were followed
31	  as written. Consolidating here is what stops that.
32	
33	Two facts fall out of comparing them:
34	
35	- **The duplication is real and drifting.** Three live,
36	  independently-edited forks — one bundling pin+overhead, one
37	  splitting them out, one minimal — is the DRY trigger the
38	  earlier "extract when a second consumer materializes" note was
39	  waiting for. We think each fork happened because there was no
40	  shared crate to depend on at the time, so each repo vendored
41	  and then edited locally.
42	- **None of the copies is `no_std`.** Every one leans on
43	  `hdrhistogram` (std), `String` probe names, and std time /
44	  `minstant`. So today nothing here can measure on an embedded
45	  target — the copies are all host-only.
46	
47	## Scope: a probe is not a harness
48	
49	The existing code already draws a clean line, worth preserving:
50	
51	- **Probe** (this crate) — read a hardware tick counter, record
52	  deltas into a named histogram, render a percentile band-table.
53	  Small, embeddable, ideally `no_std`.
54	- **Harness** (iiac-perf; zc-ring's `tp_runner`) — adaptive loop
55	  sizing, apparatus-overhead subtraction, thread pinning, a bench
56	  registry, `perf_event_open` counters. zc-ring's `tp_runner`
57	  doc says it plainly: "Deliberately not a benchmark harness …
58	  for that scale of machinery use iiac-perf."
59	
60	The consolidation target is the **probe**. The harness layer
61	(sizing, calibration, registry) stays where it is — iiac-perf
62	owns host-side characterization and already produces the
63	published percentile tables.
64	
65	## Why not an off-the-shelf benchmarking crate
66	
67	The perf-book benchmarking page [[1]] lists criterion, divan,
68	hyperfine, iai/gungraun (callgrind), bencher, and rustc-perf.
69	None replaces the probe for the need that motivates it — a
70	nanosecond-scale, cross-core, tail-latency _distribution_ of a
71	lock-free hot path, measurable on the target:
72	
73	- **criterion / divan** — host-std statistical microbench
74	  harnesses. Good for A/B + throughput at the _mean_; they do not
75	  surface deep tail bands (the n5..n10 / p.999_999_999 rows these
76	  measurements are about — batch-timing discards the
77	  distribution). Both are `std`, so neither runs on an embedded
78	  target. divan is additionally stale (last release April 2025).
79	- **iai / gungraun (callgrind)** — instruction-count / cache
80	  _simulation_. Noise-free and great for CI regression guards,
81	  but they model a single simulated run and cannot reproduce the
82	  real cross-core cache-line-transfer economics that is the whole
83	  point (the 26–40% SPSC-vs-MPSC gap zc-ring measured is a
84	  hardware effect) [[2]].
85	- **hyperfine** — whole-process wall-clock timing; wrong
86	  granularity for ns round-trips. **bencher** — a CI tracking
87	  layer, orthogonal to how the numbers are produced.
88	
89	So for _host-side_ CI regression gating an off-the-shelf harness
90	would work, but iiac-perf already covers host-side, making a
91	second one largely redundant; and for _on-target_ embedded
92	measurement nothing off-the-shelf applies at all. This
93	home-grown probe is the only thing that can measure on the
94	target — which is why it is worth investing in rather than
95	replacing.
96	
97	## The design: no_std core + std feature layer
98	
99	This crate should not be a file-move of any one fork (that just
100	relocates std code and freezes one of three designs). It should
101	be a redesign toward a `no_std` core with an opt-in `std` layer:
102	
103	- **`no_std`, no-alloc histogram** — a fixed log-linear bucket
104	  histogram replacing `hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for
105	  embedded). This is the real design nut. We think a small
106	  fixed-bucket table sized like the existing band scheme is
107	  enough for the tail bands we report; evaluate whether any
108	  existing `no_std` histogram crate fits before hand-rolling.
109	- **`&'static str` names** instead of `String`, so the core
110	  needs no allocator.
111	- **Cycle-counter abstraction** — the per-arch `ticks/`
112	  (`x86_64` `rdtsc`, `aarch64` `CNTVCT_EL0`) is already close to
113	  `no_std`; put it behind a portable-atomic-style trait so
114	  CAS-less / counter-less targets can supply their own source.
115	- **`std` feature** — the rich band-table reporting
116	  (thousands-separator formatting, tick→ns calibration display),
117	  and optionally the pinning / `perf_event_open` counters, gated
118	  so a mainstream host build keeps everything and an embedded
119	  build gets just the core.
120	- **Spans** — the span-style API exists in some copies
121	  (`tprobe_span.rs` in zc-ring, `tprobe2.rs` in iiac-perf) and
122	  not in actor-x1; fold in as an optional part of the core, same
123	  `no_std` discipline.
124	
125	## Phases: collection, analysis, presentation
126	
127	Two use classes shape the crate. In a **benchmark** the
128	measurement is the work (iiac-perf, the actor-x1 benches). In an
129	**instrumented application** the probe discovers critical paths
130	and monitors the app generally — we think this is the majority
131	use case if the crate succeeds, including embedded targets where
132	data is never presented on the device. The existing copies fold
133	analysis into collection (histogram update on the hot path,
134	rendering in-process); this design separates three phases and
135	keeps everything but raw collection off the measured path:
136	
137	- **Collection** — the hot path does a tick read, a delta, and
138	  one recorder operation; nothing else. The recorder is a
139	  pluggable trait with three `no_std` impls:
140	  - `ArrayRecorder` — preallocated delta array, one sequential
141	    store per event. For benchmarks: bounded iteration counts
142	    and a natural "after" mean no concurrent thread is needed;
143	    analysis runs after the loop with zero overlap with any
144	    measurement.
145	  - `RingRecorder` — raw deltas into an SPSC ring drained by a
146	    collector thread. For instrumented apps with spare cores:
147	    at 10M events/s the raw stream is ~80 MB/s, trivial against
148	    within-machine memory bandwidth. Shipping buffers between
149	    threads is zc-ring's domain — a natural composition, though
150	    transport stays out of this crate.
151	  - `HistogramRecorder` — in-place log-linear bucket increment.
152	    For embedded / off-machine links where raw-sample bandwidth
153	    is infeasible; the device ships fixed-size snapshots
154	    instead.
155	- **Analysis** — histogram build and percentile extraction.

... [112 lines truncated] ...

125	## Phases: collection, analysis, presentation
126	
127	Two use classes shape the crate. In a **benchmark** the
128	measurement is the work (iiac-perf, the actor-x1 benches). In an
129	**instrumented application** the probe discovers critical paths
130	and monitors the app generally — we think this is the majority
131	use case if the crate succeeds, including embedded targets where
132	data is never presented on the device. The existing copies fold
133	analysis into collection (histogram update on the hot path,
134	rendering in-process); this design separates three phases and
135	keeps everything but raw collection off the measured path:
136	
137	- **Collection** — the hot path does a tick read, a delta, and
138	  one recorder operation; nothing else. The recorder is a
139	  pluggable trait with three `no_std` impls:
140	  - `ArrayRecorder` — preallocated delta array, one sequential
141	    store per event. For benchmarks: bounded iteration counts
142	    and a natural "after" mean no concurrent thread is needed;
143	    analysis runs after the loop with zero overlap with any
144	    measurement.
145	  - `RingRecorder` — raw deltas into an SPSC ring drained by a
146	    collector thread. For instrumented apps with spare cores:
147	    at 10M events/s the raw stream is ~80 MB/s, trivial against
148	    within-machine memory bandwidth. Shipping buffers between
149	    threads is zc-ring's domain — a natural composition, though
150	    transport stays out of this crate.
151	  - `HistogramRecorder` — in-place log-linear bucket increment.
152	    For embedded / off-machine links where raw-sample bandwidth
153	    is infeasible; the device ships fixed-size snapshots
154	    instead.
155	- **Analysis** — histogram build and percentile extraction.
156	  Always off the measured path: after the loop (array), on the
157	  collector thread (ring), or off-device (histogram snapshots).
158	- **Presentation** — band-table rendering, formatting, tick→ns
159	  display. `std`-only, operates on snapshots, never on the
160	  measuring device.
161	
162	The organizing insight: **histogramming is compression, not
163	analysis**. A log-linear histogram compresses the delta
164	distribution, lossy only in bucket resolution, and it is the
165	interchange format between phases wherever compression happens.
166	The recorder choice just moves the compression point — after the
167	run, on a collector core, or on the device — so analysis and
168	presentation downstream are identical in all three modes.
169	
170	Consequences for the core:
171	
172	- **Snapshot/drain is a first-class operation** — wait-free for
173	  the recorder (double-buffered counts or an epoch swap), so a
174	  collector can harvest without blocking the hot path.
175	- **A small versioned `no_std` wire format** for histogram
176	  snapshots (bucket-layout parameters + counts) is the boundary
177	  between this crate and the world.
178	- **Transport is out of scope** — the probe produces snapshots
179	  and raw rings; which thread, socket, or UART moves them is
180	  the application's business.
181	- Raw recorders retain **time ordering** that histograms
182	  discard (correlating spikes with events, periodic patterns) —
183	  a flight-recorder use distinct from distribution measurement,
184	  and a reason the raw modes are first-class rather than merely
185	  an optimization.
186	
187	### Outer loop as cross-check, not measurement
188	
189	For the benchmark harness (iiac-perf), summing the recorded
190	inner deltas gives the duration directly, which demotes the
191	outer-loop timing from "the measurement" to a cross-check worth
192	keeping:
193	
194	- `sum(inner deltas)` — time actually spent in the measured op;
195	  the right numerator for op throughput.
196	- `outer − sum(inner)` — the per-iteration apparatus cost (tick
197	  reads, the record store, loop overhead), *measured* for that
198	  exact run. Today the copies estimate this with a separate
199	  calibration pass (actor-x1's `overhead.rs`); this makes it a
200	  free per-run self-check that calibration matches reality.
201	
202	### First experiment: recorder cost
203	
204	We think the raw array/ring store is cheaper per event than the
205	histogram increment (a store vs. leading-zeros + shift + counter
206	bump), but the histogram's table stays L1-resident while a raw
207	array streams through cache — for long runs the eviction
208	pressure could invert the ranking. Comparing recorder impls is
209	the first experiment the consolidated tprobe should run against
210	itself, folded into probe-overhead calibration.
211	
212	## Sequencing: this crate before zc-msg-x1's messaging layer
213	
214	Chosen path (decision "B" in the originating discussion):
215	consolidate `tprobe` here **before** seeding zc-msg-x1's
216	messaging layer. Seeding zc-msg on a std copy we intend to
217	replace is throwaway work, and measurement is first-class enough
218	across this repo family (embedded/on-target is a stated
219	requirement, not a nice-to-have) to justify fixing the
220	foundation first. The rejected alternative was to path-dep
221	zc-ring's existing std `tprobe` into zc-msg now and defer no_std
222	— faster to start, but it adds a fifth copy and discards work
223	later.
224	
225	## Consumers and migration
226	
227	This crate should let the live consumers converge; migration is
228	per-repo and not all at once:
229	
230	- **actor-x1** — active consumer and the probe's birthplace; a
231	  prime candidate to drop its vendored copy for this crate.
232	- **iiac-perf** — active; today it _forks_ rather than depends
233	  (uses `minstant` and its own band code). Confirm whether that
234	  fork is deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental before
235	  assuming it will depend on this crate.
236	- **zc-ring-x1** — frozen lab notebook; we think we will **not**
237	  migrate it (leave its `tprobe`/`tp_runner` as the record).
238	- **zc-msg-x1** — the new consumer; starts on this crate instead
239	  of a vendored copy.
240	
241	## Name
242	
243	Kept **`tprobe`** — already the crate name in all four repos
244	(recognition carries over), short, and "t" reads as tick/time,
245	what it counts. The only more-descriptive alternative considered
246	was `tickprobe`; the added clarity did not seem worth losing the
247	continuity.
248	
249	## Open questions
250	
251	- Which `no_std` histogram: an existing crate or a hand-rolled
252	  fixed-bucket table? Drives the core's shape.
253	- Recorder trait shape: generic (static dispatch, monomorphized
254	  per recorder) vs. an enum? Affects hot-path codegen and the
255	  API every consumer sees.
256	- Snapshot wire format: encoding, versioning, and how the
257	  bucket-layout parameters travel with the counts.
258	- Is iiac-perf's fork deliberate? Decides whether consolidation
259	  is three-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg + iiac-perf depend) or two.
260	- Where do pinning and `perf_event_open` live — in the `std`
261	  feature of this crate, or kept in a separate runner/harness
262	  crate as zc-ring split them?
263	
264	# References
265	
266	[1]: https://nnethercote.github.io/perf-book/benchmarking.html
267	[2]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1
268

file.content:
## Phases: collection, analysis, presentation

Two use classes shape the crate. In a **benchmark** the
measurement is the work (iiac-perf, the actor-x1 benches). In an
**instrumented application** the probe discovers critical paths
and monitors the app generally — we think this is the majority
use case if the crate succeeds, including embedded targets where
data is never presented on the device. The existing copies fold
analysis into collection (histogram update on the hot path,
rendering in-process); this design separates three phases and
keeps everything but raw collection off the measured path:

- **Collection** — the hot path does a tick read, a delta, and
  one recorder operation; nothing else. The recorder is a
  pluggable trait with three `no_std` impls:
  - `ArrayRecorder` — preallocated delta array, one sequential
    store per event. For benchmarks: bounded iteration counts
    and a natural "after" mean no concurrent thread is needed;
    analysis runs after the loop with zero overlap with any
    measurement.
  - `RingRecorder` — raw deltas into an SPSC ring drained by a
    collector thread. For instrumented apps with spare cores:
    at 10M events/s the raw stream is ~80 MB/s, trivial against
    within-machine memory bandwidth. Shipping buffers between
    threads is zc-ring's domain — a natural composition, though
    transport stays out of this crate.
  - `HistogramRecorder` — in-place log-linear bucket increment.
    For embedded / off-machine links where raw-sample bandwidth
    is infeasible; the device ships fixed-size snapshots
    instead.
- **Analysis** — histogram build and percentile extraction.
  Always off the measured path: after the loop (array), on the
  collector thread (ring), or off-device (histogram snapshots).
- **Presentation** — band-table rendering, formatting, tick→ns
  display. `std`-only, operates on snapshots, never on the
  measuring device.

The organizing insight: **histogramming is compression, not
analysis**. A log-linear histogram compresses the delta
distribution, lossy only in bucket resolution, and it is the
interchange format between phases wherever compression happens.
The recorder choice just moves the compression point — after the
run, on a collector core, or on the device — so analysis and
presentation downstream are identical in all three modes.

Consequences for the core:

- **Snapshot/drain is a first-class operation** — wait-free for
  the recorder (double-buffered counts or an epoch swap), so a
  collector can harvest without blocking the hot path.
- **A small versioned `no_std` wire format** for histogram
  snapshots (bucket-layout parameters + counts) is the boundary
  between this crate and the world.
- **Transport is out of scope** — the probe produces snapshots
  and raw rings; which thread, socket, or UART moves them is
  the application's business.
- Raw recorders retain **time ordering** that histograms
  discard (correlating spikes with events, periodic patterns) —
  a flight-recorder use distinct from distribution measurement,
  and a reason the raw modes are first-class rather than merely
  an optimization.

### Outer loop as cross-check, not measurement

For the benchmark harness (iiac-perf), summing the recorded
inner deltas gives the duration directly, which demotes the
outer-loop timing from "the measurement" to a cross-check worth
keeping:

- `sum(inner deltas)` — time actually spent in the measured op;
  the right numerator for op throughput.
- `outer − sum(inner)` — the per-iteration apparatus cost (tick
  reads, the record store, loop overhead), *measured* for that
  exact run. Today the copies estimate this with a separate
  calibration pass (actor-x1's `overhead.rs`); this makes it a
  free per-run self-check that calibration matches reality.

### First experiment: recorder cost

We think the raw array/ring store is cheaper per event than the
histogram increment (a store vs. leading-zeros + shift + counter
bump), but the histogram's table stays L1-resident while a raw
array streams through cache — for long runs the eviction
pressure could invert the ranking. Comparing recorder impls is
the first experiment the consolidated tprobe should run against
itself, folded into probe-overhead calibration.

## Sequencing: this crate before zc-msg-x1's messaging layer

Chosen path (decision "B" in the originating discussion):
consolidate `tprobe` here **before** seeding zc-msg-x1's
messaging layer. Seeding zc-msg on a std copy we intend to
replace is throwaway work, and measurement is first-class enough
across this repo family (embedded/on-target is a stated
requirement, not a nice-to-have) to justify fixing the
foundation first. The rejected alternative was to path-dep
zc-ring's existing std `tprobe` into zc-msg now and defer no_std
— faster to start, but it adds a fifth copy and discards work
later.

## Consumers and migration

This crate should let the live consumers converge; migration is
per-repo and not all at once:

- **actor-x1** — active consumer and the probe's birthplace; a
  prime candidate to drop its vendored copy for this crate.
- **iiac-perf** — active; today it _forks_ rather than depends
  (uses `minstant` and its own band code). Confirm whether that
  fork is deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental before
  assuming it will depend on this crate.
- **zc-ring-x1** — frozen lab notebook; we think we will **not**
  migrate it (leave its `tprobe`/`tp_runner` as the record).
- **zc-msg-x1** — the new consumer; starts on this crate instead
  of a vendored copy.

## Name

Kept **`tprobe`** — already the crate name in all four repos
(recognition carries over), short, and "t" reads as tick/time,
what it counts. The only more-descriptive alternative considered
was `tickprobe`; the added clarity did not seem worth losing the
continuity.

## Open questions

- Which `no_std` histogram: an existing crate or a hand-rolled
  fixed-bucket table? Drives the core's shape.
- Recorder trait shape: generic (static dispatch, monomorphized
  per recorder) vs. an enum? Affects hot-path codegen and the
  API every consumer sees.
- Snapshot wire format: encoding, versioning, and how the
  bucket-layout parameters travel with the counts.
- Is iiac-perf's fork deliberate? Decides whether consolidation
  is three-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg + iiac-perf depend) or two.
- Where do pinning and `perf_event_open` live — in the `std`
  feature of this crate, or kept in a separate runner/harness
  crate as zc-ring split them?

# References

[1]: https://nnethercote.github.io/perf-book/benchmarking.html
[2]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1

content.text:
Reread — the edits read well (tightened intro clause in Phases, plus the smaller touches).

On unwrap: I agree with the lean, and for this crate specifically the bar should be even higher than usual. My thoughts, organized:

**The siblings are two different risks, worth separating.**

- `.unwrap()` / `.expect()` — **panic** risk. In a library it punishes the consumer; in a `no_std` embedded build a panic is typically an abort/halt, and it also drags panic-formatting machinery into the binary. Worst case for tprobe's core.
- `.unwrap_or(…)` / `_or_default()` / `_or_else(…)` — no panic, but a **silently substituted value**. Arguably sneakier: the program keeps running with a number that might be wrong, and nothing tells you. A measurement crate quietly defaulting a value is a data-integrity bug, not a crash — harder to notice. So including them in the alert policy is right.

**The legitimate-use taxonomy I'd apply** (what I'll cite when alerting):

1. *Locally provable unreachability* — e.g., an arm guard guarantees `len() == 1`. Legit, but the first question is always whether a restructure (match, `if let`, slice patterns) lets the type system prove it instead — the unwrap-free version is usually also the clearer version.
2. *Infallible-by-signature APIs* — e.g., `write!` into a `String`. Legit-ish, but propagating `fmt::Result` is nearly free, so often not even worth it.
3. *Startup/config, fail-fast intended* — an `expect` with a good message is honest. Rare in a library; mostly a bin/harness pattern.
4. *Defaults that are semantically real* (`-v` absent → Info). Fine, but the default should be visible and deliberate, which the `// OK:` comment forces.

**tprobe-specific: the best unwrap policy is API shapes that never produce an `Option`/`Result` on the hot path.** `record(delta)` should be infallible *by construction* — histogram bucket index saturates into the top/bottom bucket, ring full has an explicit policy (overwrite or drop-and-count, a design choice worth its own bullet later), array full drops-and-counts. Then the question mostly evaporates where it matters most.

**Mechanical enforcement beats my memory.** Rather than relying on me to remember to alert you, enable the lints AGENTS.md already anticipates — in `Cargo.toml`:

```toml
[lints.clippy]
unwrap_used = "warn"
expect_used = "warn"
```

Then every panicking site in non-test code *requires* an explicit `#[allow]` + `// OK:` at the site — it shows up in the diff, clippy (which runs in preflight) catches any I miss, and your review naturally becomes the alert point. The `_or*` siblings have no clippy lint, so those stay covered by the existing `// OK:` comment convention plus me flagging them in conversation.

Want me to (a) add the lints to `Cargo.toml`, and (b) record the alert policy in AGENTS.md (a short addition to the existing `// OK:` section: prefer restructuring over `unwrap*` in non-test code; when one is introduced, call it out in conversation for review)?
