content.content:
1	//! Free-form measurement probe: a named, single-writer histogram
2	//! of hardware tick-counter deltas.
3	//!
4	//! Same shape as [`crate::probe::Probe`], but the caller records
5	//! tick deltas (`ticks::read_ticks() − ticks::read_ticks()`)
6	//! rather than nanoseconds. Skipping the tick→ns conversion at
7	//! record time trims a mul-shift from the hot path; conversion
8	//! to nanoseconds, if desired, is deferred to the report phase
9	//! using [`crate::ticks::ticks_per_ns`].
10	//!
11	//! For a scope-based recording API (`start` / `end` with a
12	//! deferred-processing record buffer) see [`crate::tprobe2`].
13	//! The two primitives are kept separate because the scope API's
14	//! buffer-per-sample model trades hot-path throughput for
15	//! flexibility, so mixing the paths on one type forced awkward
16	//! trade-offs.
17	
18	use hdrhistogram::Histogram;
19	
20	use crate::band_table;
21	use crate::ticks;
22	
23	/// A named, single-writer histogram of hardware tick-counter
24	/// deltas. Not `Sync`; cross-thread *sharing* is out of scope.
25	/// `Send` so probes can be moved between threads (e.g. returned
26	/// via a `JoinHandle<TProbe>` on shutdown).
27	pub struct TProbe {
28	    name: String,
29	    hist: Histogram<u64>,
30	}
31	
32	impl TProbe {
33	    /// Create an empty probe. Histogram upper bound is 1e12
34	    /// ticks (~250 s at 4 GHz, ~100 s at 10 GHz), 3 significant
35	    /// figures.
36	    ///
37	    /// Exits the process (code 1) if the hardware tick counter
38	    /// isn't usable — see [`crate::ticks::require_ok`].
39	    pub fn new(name: &str) -> Self {
40	        ticks::require_ok();
41	        // Trigger calibration eagerly so the first report() doesn't
42	        // pay for it.
43	        let _ = ticks::ticks_per_ns();
44	        Self {
45	            name: name.to_string(),
46	            hist: Histogram::<u64>::new_with_bounds(1, 1_000_000_000_000, 3).unwrap(),
47	        }
48	    }
49	
50	    /// Record a single sample, in tick-counter deltas. Values
51	    /// of 0 are clamped to 1 since the histogram's lower bound
52	    /// is 1; back-to-back tick reads can produce 0 on fast cores.
53	    pub fn record(&mut self, ticks: u64) {
54	        self.hist.record(ticks.max(1)).unwrap();
55	    }
56	
57	    /// Render a band-table report for this probe. `as_ticks`
58	    /// controls the display unit: `false` converts stored tick
59	    /// deltas to nanoseconds (default for the CLI); `true` shows
60	    /// raw ticks (`-t`/`--ticks`).
61	    pub fn report(&self, as_ticks: bool) {
62	        band_table::render("tprobe", &self.name, &self.hist, as_ticks);
63	    }
64	}
65

content.content:
1	//! Hardware tick counter abstraction: thin wrapper over the
2	//! target architecture's fixed-rate monotonic counter.
3	//!
4	//! Probes call three functions; the per-arch impl lives in a
5	//! child module gated by `#[cfg(target_arch = ...)]`:
6	//!
7	//! - [`read_ticks`] — current counter value.
8	//! - [`ticks_per_ns`] — calibrated conversion ratio.
9	//! - [`require_ok`] — exit the process if the counter isn't
10	//!   usable for probe measurements.
11	//!
12	//! `x86_64` (`rdtsc`) and `aarch64` (`CNTVCT_EL0`) are
13	//! implemented today. RISC-V (`time` CSR) has an architecturally
14	//! invariant counter by ISA spec, so its `require_ok` will be
15	//! (nearly) a no-op once that impl lands.
16	
17	#[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
18	mod x86_64;
19	
20	#[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
21	use x86_64 as imp;
22	
23	#[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]
24	mod aarch64;
25	
26	#[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]
27	use aarch64 as imp;
28	
29	#[cfg(not(any(target_arch = "x86_64", target_arch = "aarch64")))]
30	compile_error!(
31	    "iiac-perf probes currently only support target_arch = \"x86_64\" \
32	     and \"aarch64\". Add a per-arch impl module (RISC-V: time CSR) \
33	     and wire it into src/ticks.rs."
34	);
35	
36	/// Read the current tick counter. Monotonic and fixed-rate.
37	#[inline(always)]
38	pub fn read_ticks() -> u64 {
39	    imp::read_ticks()
40	}
41	
42	/// Conversion ratio: counter ticks per nanosecond. Calibrated
43	/// (x86_64) or read from hardware (aarch64, `CNTFRQ_EL0`).
44	/// Cached — the first call does the work.
45	pub fn ticks_per_ns() -> f64 {
46	    imp::ticks_per_ns()
47	}
48	
49	/// Verify the tick counter is usable for probe measurements;
50	/// exit the process (code 1) with a diagnostic if not. The
51	/// checks performed depend on the target architecture.
52	pub fn require_ok() {
53	    imp::require_ok();
54	}
55

[package]
name = "iiac-perf"
version = "0.20.0"
edition = "2024"

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: no_std tprobe core**

[design.md](design.md) settles tprobe's founding rationale and
the collection / analysis / presentation phase split (pluggable
Recorder, histogram-as-compression), but leaves open questions
that gate the crate's shape: which `no_std` histogram, the
recorder trait shape, the snapshot wire format, whether
iiac-perf's fork is deliberate, and where pinning /
`perf_event_open` live. Resolve the gating ones — recording
each decision in a chores design subsection — then seed the
`no_std` core from the existing copies under the new
constraints (no-alloc histogram, `&'static str` names,
cycle-counter trait, recorders, `std`-gated reporting).

- 0.1.0-0 prep: open cycle + chores design subsections for the
  three questions (done)
- 0.1.0-1 docs: no_std core phase split + unwrap lints (done)
- 0.1.0-2 decide `no_std` histogram — existing crate vs
  hand-rolled fixed-bucket table [[1]]
- 0.1.0-3 confirm iiac-perf fork intent — deliberate (two-repo)
  vs incidental (three-repo consolidation) [[2]]
- 0.1.0-4 place pinning / `perf_event_open` — this crate's `std`
  feature vs a separate runner crate [[3]]
- 0.1.0-5 seed the core from the existing copies per the
  phase/recorder design (ticks, probe, recorder trait);
  expand into a sub-cycle as it grows
- 0.1.0 close-out and validation

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

# References

[1]: chores/chores-01.md#q1-no_std-histogram-crate-vs-hand-rolled
[2]: chores/chores-01.md#q2-iiac-perf-fork-deliberate
[3]: chores/chores-01.md#q3-pinning-and-perf_event_open-placement

# no_std-first hardware tick-counter measurement probe.
# Consolidates four drifted copies (actor-x1, zc-ring-x1,
# iiac-perf, prospectively zc-msg-x1) into one crate; see
# notes/design.md for the founding rationale.
[package]
name = "tprobe"
version = "0.1.0-1"
edition = "2024"
rust-version = "1.97"
license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"
description = "no_std-first hardware tick-counter measurement probe"
publish = false

[features]
default = ["std"]
std = []

# Panicking sites are opt-in: each needs a site `#[allow]` +
# `// OK: …` justification (see AGENTS.md > Code Conventions).
[lints.clippy]
unwrap_used = "warn"
expect_used = "warn"

[dependencies]

+//! Reads a hardware tick counter and records deltas through a
+//! pluggable [`Recorder`] (collection phase); analysis and
+//! presentation happen off the measured path — see the "Phases"
+//! section of `notes/design.md`:
-//! - the core is no-alloc and `no_std`, embeddable on-target
-//! - the `std` feature (default) adds host-side reporting,
-//!   tick→ns calibration display, and optionally pinning /
-//!   `perf_event_open` counters
+//! - [`ticks`] — per-arch tick counter (`rdtsc`,
+//!   `CNTVCT_EL0`); reads are `no_std`, calibration and
+//!   validation are `std`-gated
+//! - [`Recorder`] / [`ArrayRecorder`] — where deltas go;
+//!   no-alloc, embeddable on-target
+//! - [`TProbe`] — the collection front end
+//!   (`start()` … `record()`)
//! Founding rationale and design live in `notes/design.md`. The

contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

[design.md](design.md) settles tprobe's founding rationale and
the collection / analysis / presentation phase split (pluggable
Recorder, histogram-as-compression), but leaves open questions
that gate the crate's shape: which `no_std` histogram, the
recorder trait shape, the snapshot wire format, whether
iiac-perf's fork is deliberate, and where pinning /
`perf_event_open` live. Resolve the gating ones — recording
each decision in a chores design subsection — then seed the
`no_std` core from the existing copies under the new
constraints (no-alloc histogram, `&'static str` names,
cycle-counter trait, recorders, `std`-gated reporting).

content.text:
Work complete — ready for your review. What landed (`0.1.0-2`):

**Crate core (all `no_std`-clean; `cargo clippy --no-default-features` passes):**
- `src/ticks.rs` + `src/ticks/{x86_64,aarch64}.rs` — seeded from iiac-perf; `read_ticks` is core, `ticks_per_ns`/`check` are `std`-gated. Two deliberate changes: calibration via `std::time::Instant` (drops `minstant`), and `check() -> Result` instead of exiting the process — the example does the exit (library discipline).
- `src/recorder.rs` — `Recorder` trait (record must be infallible by construction) + `ArrayRecorder<B>`: storage-generic (`&mut [u64]`, arrays, `Vec`), drop-and-count on full.
- `src/probe.rs` — `TProbe<R>` with your API: start tick stored in the probe, `start()`/`record()`, plus `read_ticks()` method and a `record_delta()` escape hatch.
- 9 unit tests; `clippy.toml` allows unwrap/expect in tests per the convention.

**`examples/tp_pc/`** — parity port: identical loop/channels/band-table; recording is the one variable. Buffer is pre-faulted so page faults don't land in the measured loop. `hdrhistogram` + `core_affinity` are dev-deps only.

**Unwrap-policy alerts** — the sites I introduced, all in non-test code, for your review:
1. `x86_64.rs` — `.unwrap_or(false)` on the sysfs clocksource read: unreadable sysfs → treat as not-tsc and refuse. Fail-closed default.
2. `examples/tp_pc/band_table.rs` (copied code) — 2× `.unwrap_or(n_bands-1)` (mid-rank ≥ last boundary → top band), 6× width `.max().unwrap_or(0)` (empty rows), 1× `.unwrap_or(0)` in `fmt_commas_f64` (non-finite floats).
3. `examples/tp_pc/main.rs` — 2× `#[allow] .unwrap()` on hdrhistogram (constant bounds; values clamped into bounds) and 2× `#[allow] .expect()` on thread joins (propagate child panic; fail-fast correct in a bench).

Diff is in the working copy (`jj diff`); smoke outputs above. Please review — commit description comes next on your go.

message.content: Actually, there isn't any real difference between TProbeSpan and TProbe other than the site_id and maybe we should have only one?

content.text:
Yes — and the reason the two types existed is instructive. iiac-perf's `tprobe.rs` doc justifies the split explicitly: "the scope API's buffer-per-sample model trades hot-path throughput for flexibility, so mixing the paths on one type forced awkward trade-offs." That tension was real in the old design because `TProbe` compressed inline (histogram) while `TProbe2` buffered records. **The Recorder abstraction dissolves exactly that** — both now buffer through a recorder, so the old reason for two types is gone.

What actually remains of "span" after unification is two small add-ons, neither of which needs a second probe type:

1. **Interleaved / non-stack scopes.** Probe-stored start supports one outstanding scope; `TProbe2`'s trick is that the start tick travels in a caller-held token. That's just an alternate method pair on the same probe — `start_span() -> Span` / `record_span(span)` — both funneling into the same recorder. The token is a `Copy` value; no extra state in the probe.

2. **site_id / payload shape.** This is really a *sample-type* question, not a probe question: `u64` delta (what we have), `(site_id, delta)`, or `(site_id, start, end)` for time-ordered traces (the flight-recorder value the design section already notes raw recorders have). That generalizes `Recorder` over its sample type someday — `Recorder<S = u64>` — and the probe front end stays one type.

So: one `TProbe`, span methods as a later ladder step, payload generality deferred until a consumer actually wants site-tagged records (zc-msg is the likely first). I'd record the decision now though, since it retires a design.md bullet — the "Spans" bullet currently says "fold in as an optional part of the core," which under-states what we just concluded. Proposed rewrite of that bullet:

```
- **Spans** — not a second probe type. The old copies split
  TProbe/TProbe2 (zc-ring's `tprobe_span.rs`) because one
  histogrammed inline and one buffered records; the Recorder
  split dissolves that reason. What remains of "span" folds
  into the one probe later:
  - a caller-held start token (`start_span() -> Span`,
    `record_span(Span)`) for interleaved / non-stack scopes,
    where the probe-stored start can't serve;
  - a richer sample payload (`(site_id, delta)`, or
    `(site_id, start, end)` for time-ordered traces) — a
    Recorder sample-type question (`Recorder<S = u64>`), not a
    probe question.
```

Want me to fold that into `notes/design.md` as part of this commit (it's design rationale that belongs with the seeding step), and leave the span methods as a new ladder entry after the comparison step?

message.content: I think we do want to do that. But I think span, as currently implemented is weird, we have one set of records and one histogram but multiple sites, so site_id doesn't seem to be useful.

content.text:
Exactly — the site_id in `tprobe2.rs` is literally `#[allow(dead_code)] // read once per-site grouping lands.` today: one buffer, one histogram, many sites, and every record drains into the same histogram regardless of id. The "per-site grouping" it was waiting for is something the probe model already gives for free: **a probe per site, where the `&'static str` name *is* the site.** Per-site histograms, per-site reports, no id plumbing — and cross-site interleaving stops being a problem because each probe holds its own start tick.

That strengthens the conclusion: span doesn't fold in, it *dissolves* —

- the two-type split dies with the Recorder abstraction (the buffer-vs-inline-histogram tension is gone),
- site_id dies with probe-per-site,
- what's genuinely left is only: (a) overlapping scopes *on one probe* (same site concurrently active — rare once probes are per-site; a caller-held `Span` token, added when a consumer actually needs it), and (b) time-ordered cross-site tracing — which is a *recorder sample-type* question (`(id, start, end)` trace records, the flight-recorder use the Phases section already notes), not a probe-API question.

Updating the design.md bullet to record that:

message.content: ATM there are no users of TProbe::read_ticks is there any anticipated users?

content.text:
Honest answer: only one, and it's not here yet. The rationale I wrote ("callers that manage their own tick math stay on one source") is served equally well today by the free `ticks::read_ticks()` — the method is a pure pass-through. The method only becomes *necessary* at the cycle-counter-trait step (design.md's portable tick-source abstraction): once `TProbe<C: TickSource, R>` owns which source it reads, `probe.read_ticks()` is the only way to read *that probe's* source, and the free fn can't know. Until then it's dead API surface.

So: drop it now, re-add when the trait makes it meaningful — removing is free, and the trait step will reshape this signature anyway. `record_delta` I'd keep: it has a near-term user (ports from the old `record(ticks)` API during consumer migration, and the shared-one-read-across-probes pattern). Removing:

message.content: What about record_delta and why into_recorder?

content.text:
Same test applied honestly:

- **`record_delta`** — weaker than I claimed. A caller managing its own tick reads doesn't actually need `TProbe` at all: `Recorder::record` is public, so raw-delta recording is just `ArrayRecorder` used directly — the probe's *entire* value-add is the start/record tick handling. And a migrating consumer (actor-x1) would adopt `start()`/`record()` rather than keep its tick locals. No concrete user → drop.
- **`into_recorder`** — the "collection ended, hand storage to analysis" seam, but it's convenience, not necessity: the probe itself is `Send` when its recorder is, so shipping results means moving the probe; and in-place reading is `recorder()` — which is what the example actually uses. The genuine future user is buffer *reuse* across runs (take the `Vec` back out) — when that lands, re-add. Drop.

That trims the probe to exactly its used surface: `new` / `start` / `record` / `name` / `recorder`.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: no_std tprobe core**

[design.md](design.md) settles tprobe's founding rationale and
the collection / analysis / presentation phase split (pluggable
Recorder, histogram-as-compression), but leaves open questions
that gate the crate's shape: which `no_std` histogram, the
recorder trait shape, the snapshot wire format, whether
iiac-perf's fork is deliberate, and where pinning /
`perf_event_open` live. Resolve the gating ones — recording
each decision in a chores design subsection — then seed the
`no_std` core from the existing copies under the new
constraints (no-alloc histogram, `&'static str` names,
cycle-counter trait, recorders, `std`-gated reporting).

- 0.1.0-0 prep: open cycle + chores design subsections for the
  three questions (done)
- 0.1.0-1 docs: no_std core phase split + unwrap lints (done)
- 0.1.0-2 feat: seed ticks + ArrayRecorder + TProbe core, with
  `examples/tp_pc` as a parity port of iiac-perf's `tp-pc`
  (same loop, channels, band-table output) (done)
- 0.1.0-3 run the tp_pc comparison vs iiac-perf's `tp-pc`;
  record cost/benefit findings in a chores design subsection
- 0.1.0-4 decide `no_std` histogram — existing crate vs
  hand-rolled fixed-bucket table [[1]]
- 0.1.0-5 confirm iiac-perf fork intent — deliberate (two-repo)
  vs incidental (three-repo consolidation) [[2]]
- 0.1.0-6 place pinning / `perf_event_open` — this crate's `std`
  feature vs a separate runner crate [[3]]
- 0.1.0 close-out and validation

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

[2]: chores/chores-01.md#q2-iiac-perf-fork-deliberate
[3]: chores/chores-01.md#q3-pinning-and-perf_event_open-placement

aiTitle: Separate data collection from analysis and presentation

content.text:
Proposed commit description:

**Title:** `feat: seed no_std core + tp_pc parity example`

**Body:**
```
Seed the collection phase from iiac-perf under the new design
constraints (no_std core, pluggable Recorder, analysis off the
measured path), with examples/tp_pc as a parity port of
iiac-perf's tp-pc bench — same loop, channels, and band-table
output; only the recording differs (raw-delta array store vs
hdrhistogram record at collection).

- src/ticks.rs, src/ticks/x86_64.rs, src/ticks/aarch64.rs:
  seeded from iiac-perf; read_ticks is no_std, ticks_per_ns /
  check are std-gated; calibration via std::time::Instant
  (drops the minstant dep); check returns Result instead of
  exiting the process — the binary decides
- src/recorder.rs: Recorder trait (record is infallible by
  construction) + ArrayRecorder<B> — storage-generic
  (&mut [u64] / array / Vec), drop-and-count when full
- src/probe.rs: TProbe<R> — start tick stored in the probe;
  start()/record(); name()/recorder() accessors
- src/lib.rs: wire modules, re-export TProbe / Recorder /
  ArrayRecorder; crate doc updated
- examples/tp_pc/main.rs: parity bench — pinning, pre-faulted
  delta buffers, post-run histogram build + report
- examples/tp_pc/band_table.rs: copy of iiac-perf's renderer
  (+ fmt_commas helpers) so output is line-for-line comparable
- Cargo.toml: version 0.1.0-2; hdrhistogram + core_affinity
  as dev-dependencies (example-only)
- Cargo.lock: dev-dependency additions
- clippy.toml: allow unwrap/expect in tests, per convention
- notes/design.md: Spans bullet rewritten — spans dissolve
  (Recorder removes the two-type split; site_id dies with
  probe-per-site; span token + trace payload deferred)
- notes/todo.md: ladder reshaped — -2 this step, -3 the
  comparison run, questions renumbered -4..-6
```

(`ochid:` injected by the push.) On your approval I'll run `vc-x1 push main` and stop there; the `-3` comparison runs come next turn.

content.text:
No — none of them are in tests, actually (the tests happened to need none). But they split into two very different classes:

**Panicking sites (`unwrap()`/`expect()`) — 4, all in the example binary, none in the library:**
- `main.rs:120` `Histogram::new_with_bounds(...).unwrap()` — constant bounds, can't fail
- `main.rs:124` `hist.record(clamped).unwrap()` — value clamped into bounds, can't fail
- `main.rs:201,204` `join().expect(...)` — propagate a child-thread panic; fail-fast is right in a bench

Each carries `#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used/expect_used)]` + `// OK:` — the lints would reject them otherwise. All four are in the *analysis/report phase* of the example, after measurement ends.

**Silent-default siblings (`unwrap_or`) — 12, no panic path:**
- 1 in the **library** (`x86_64.rs:88`): unreadable sysfs → `false` → `check()` refuses to run. Fails closed — the conservative direction.
- 10 in `band_table.rs` — the verbatim copy of iiac-perf's renderer (band-index top-band fallback ×2, column-width-of-empty-rows ×6 — those wouldn't even be reached since the function returns early on `sample_count == 0`, kept as-is to stay a faithful copy — and the non-finite-float fallback in `fmt_commas_f64`).
- 1 more of the same pattern at `band_table.rs:91` (the label-width chain).

So the library core proper has **zero** panicking sites and one fail-closed fallback; everything else lives in the example, and 10 of those 12 `unwrap_or`s are inherited verbatim from the iiac-perf copy rather than newly authored. If you'd rather the copied renderer be brought up to the restructure-first standard, that's worth doing when it graduates from example-local copy to the crate's real `std` reporting (post-Q1) rather than now — it's throwaway parity scaffolding.

message.content: convince me the unwrap_or(false) in kernel_clocksource_is_tsc() it worth while?

content.text:
I can't, actually — trying to defend it shows it's the wrong form. The *semantics* are right (fail closed: if we can't verify the kernel trusts the TSC, refuse to measure), but the `unwrap_or(false)` is a default-substitution combinator doing the job of a predicate. Rust has the exact shape this wants:

```rust
.is_ok_and(|s| s.trim() == "tsc")
```

That says "the read succeeded **and** says tsc" directly — no substituted value, nothing for a reader to double-check, and it's precisely the "prefer a shape that doesn't need them" restructure the policy asks for first. My `// OK:` comment was justifying a form the code didn't need. Fixing:

std::fs::read_to_string("/sys/devices/system/clocksource/clocksource0/current_clocksource")
        .map(|s| s.trim() == "tsc")
        .unwrap_or(false) // OK: unreadable sysfs → treat as not-tsc and refuse

        .is_ok_and(|s| s.trim() == "tsc")

I think it can be a function closure passing the appropriate r.xxx.len() and don't use unwrap_or.

content.text: Agreed — and your experience ("I had to investigate each one") is exactly the argument: six identical `// OK: obvious` comments meant six separate reader-verifications of the same fact. Repetition of an identical unwrap pattern is a missing helper, and once it's a helper the `unwrap_or` can disappear entirely: `fold(0, usize::max)` computes a max-with-floor directly — no `Option`, no default substitution, nothing to justify.

content.text: Good instinct, one collision to flag: `band_count` is already taken in that function — it's the per-band sample-count vector (`band_first` / `band_last` / `band_count` / `band_sum`), so renaming `n_bands → band_count` would collide, and `band_last_idx` would sit confusingly next to the existing `band_last` vec. Same intent, collision-free naming: keep `n_bands`, name the repeated expression `top_band_idx` — it's semantically the index of the top (p99-max) band, which is exactly what all five `n_bands - 1` uses mean (fall into the top band ×2, exclude the top band ×2, compare against it ×1):

message.content: nice, please change line 218 to not use `unwrap_or(0)` I want to see how it looks

content.text: That one's the best demo of the lot, because restructuring doesn't just *look* different — it exposes that `unwrap_or(0)` was quietly producing wrong output: a non-finite value (`inf`/`NaN`) formats as `"inf"`, fails the parse, and got substituted with `0` — so infinity rendered as `"0"`. The match shape hands the unparseable text through instead:

input.old_string:
let int_num: u64 = int_part.parse().unwrap_or(0); // OK: non-finite formats (inf/NaN) have no integer part
    format!("{sign}{}{frac_part}", fmt_commas(int_num))

lines: Some(i) => (&body[..i], &body[i..]),
None => (body, ""),
-    let int_num: u64 = int_part.parse().unwrap_or(0); // OK: non-finite formats (inf/NaN) have no integer part
-    format!("{sign}{}{frac_part}", fmt_commas(int_num))
+    let int_commas = match int_part.parse::<u64>() {
+        Ok(n) => fmt_commas(n),
+        // Non-finite values format as "inf"/"NaN" — no integer
+        // part to comma-group; pass the text through as-is.
+        Err(_) => int_part.to_string(),
+    format!("{sign}{int_commas}{frac_part}")

message.content: Let's run the -3 comparison, I suggest a 300s run, will that make the comparison more consistent?

content.text:
300 s helps, but mostly for the tails: at the observed ~113 K samples/s that's ~34 M samples per thread, which resolves out past p99.9999 instead of the smoke run's p99.9 — and means/stdev tighten accordingly. Two things matter *more* for making the comparison consistent, so I'll fold them in:

- **Interleaved order (A-B-B-A)** — one long run of each still confounds "tprobe vs iiac-perf" with "cold vs warm machine". Running iiac→tprobe→tprobe→iiac cancels slow drift (thermal, frequency) to first order.
- **`--cap` must exceed the sample count** — 34 M > the default 8 M, and once the array fills, `record()` degenerates to a counter bump: the loop keeps running but the hot path *changes* mid-run, quietly invalidating the comparison. I'll use `--cap 50000000` (400 MB/thread, 800 MB total — fine on this box).

One residual difference to note for the record: iiac-perf takes a systemd sleep-inhibit and runs its calibration pass; ours doesn't (sleep-inhibit only prevents suspend, not a perf setting, so it shouldn't move numbers — noted as a "we think" in the findings).

Marking `-3` current and launching the ~21-minute A-B-B-A sequence in the background:

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: no_std tprobe core**

[design.md](design.md) settles tprobe's founding rationale and
the collection / analysis / presentation phase split (pluggable
Recorder, histogram-as-compression), but leaves open questions
that gate the crate's shape: which `no_std` histogram, the
recorder trait shape, the snapshot wire format, whether
iiac-perf's fork is deliberate, and where pinning /
`perf_event_open` live. Resolve the gating ones — recording
each decision in a chores design subsection — then seed the
`no_std` core from the existing copies under the new
constraints (no-alloc histogram, `&'static str` names,
cycle-counter trait, recorders, `std`-gated reporting).

  vs incidental (three-repo consolidation) [[2]]
- 0.1.0-6 place pinning / `perf_event_open` — this crate's `std`
  feature vs a separate runner crate [[3]]

#!/bin/bash
# A-B-B-A comparison: A = iiac-perf tp-pc (histogram at
# collection), B = tprobe examples/tp_pc (ArrayRecorder).
# 300 s each, cores 2,3; interleaved order cancels slow drift.
set -u
IIAC=/home/wink/data/prgs/rust/iiac-perf/target/release/iiac-perf
TP=/home/wink/data/prgs/rust/tprobe/target/release/examples/tp_pc

run_a() { echo "=== $1: iiac-perf tp-pc ($(date +%T)) ==="; "$IIAC" tp-pc --duration 300 --pin 2,3; }
run_b() { echo "=== $1: tprobe tp_pc ($(date +%T)) ==="; "$TP" --secs 300 --cores 2,3 --cap 50000000; }

run_a A1
run_b B1
run_b B2
run_a A2
echo "=== done ($(date +%T)) ==="

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## feat: no_std tprobe core

Commits:

[design.md](../design.md) is the founding record; it leaves
three open questions [[1]] that set the crate's shape. This
cycle resolves them — recording each decision in a `###`
subsection below — then stands up the `no_std` core.

- Scope is the **probe** only — read a tick counter, record
  deltas into a named histogram, render a percentile band-table.
  The harness layer (loop sizing, overhead calibration, bench
  registry) stays in iiac-perf.
- Each open question below is a ladder step; the step lands the
  decision and this subsection records the outcome and its
  reasoning.

### Q1 no_std histogram crate vs hand-rolled

The core needs a `no_std`, no-alloc histogram to replace
`hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for embedded) — the real design
nut. We think a small fixed log-linear bucket table, sized like
the existing band scheme, is enough for the tail bands we report
(n5..n10 / p.999_999_999). Open: adopt an existing `no_std`
histogram crate, or hand-roll the fixed-bucket table. Evaluate
existing crates before hand-rolling; the choice drives the
core's shape.

### Q2 iiac-perf fork deliberate

iiac-perf today *forks* the probe rather than depending on it —
it uses `minstant` and its own band code. Open: is that fork
deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental. Deliberate →
consolidation is two-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg depend); incidental
→ three-repo (iiac-perf depends too). Confirm before assuming
which shape we are building for.

### Q3 pinning and perf_event_open placement

CPU pinning (`pin.rs`) and Linux `perf_event_open` counters sit
in different places across the copies — folded into the probe in
actor-x1, split into a separate `tp_runner` crate in zc-ring.
Open: put them behind this crate's `std` feature, or keep them
in a separate runner/harness crate as zc-ring split them. We
think the core stays `no_std` either way; this only decides
where the host-only machinery attaches.

# References

[1]: /notes/design.md#open-questions

input.old_string: ### Q2 iiac-perf fork deliberate
input.new_string:
### tp_pc parity comparison: ArrayRecorder vs hdrhistogram

`examples/tp_pc` (tprobe `TProbe` + `ArrayRecorder`, raw-delta
store) vs iiac-perf's `tp-pc` (hdrhistogram record at
collection), identical loop / channels / band-table. Four
300 s runs, interleaved A-B-B-A (A = iiac-perf, B = tprobe) on
cores 2,3 to cancel slow drift; `--cap 50M` so the array never
fills. Raw output:
[tp-pc-abba-300s-2026-07-12.txt](../tp-pc-abba-300s-2026-07-12.txt).
Producer-loop summary:

| run | count | mean | mean min-p99 | p99-max mean |
|-----|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| A1  | 34,463,849 | 8,620 ns | 8,541 ns | 16,775 ns |
| B1  | 35,214,509 | 8,507 ns | 8,475 ns | 11,882 ns |
| B2  | 35,914,748 | 8,340 ns | 8,309 ns | 11,677 ns |
| A2  | 35,222,140 | 8,432 ns | 8,400 ns | 11,820 ns |

Findings:

- **ArrayRecorder measures ~1.2% lower means and ~2.1% higher
  throughput.** The drift-cancelling half-estimates agree —
  A−B mean 113 ns (A1−B1) and 92 ns (A2−B2), ~102 ns average;
  counts +0.75 M / +0.69 M per 300 s. We think the agreement
  across both pairs makes this a real effect, not drift.
- **~87 ns per record.** Round-trip period 8.611 µs (A) vs
  8.437 µs (B): 174 ns per iteration across the two records
  (one per thread). We think hdrhistogram's record path is
  the difference — larger than a naive bucket-increment
  estimate; the controlled recorder-cost experiment
  ([design.md](../design.md#first-experiment-recorder-cost))
  should confirm the mechanism before we lean on it.
- **The peer's recording cost lands inside the measured
  window.** We think that's why the *means* moved and not just
  throughput: in a ping-pong loop each thread's delta includes
  waiting for its peer, whose recording happens during that
  wait.
- **Drift is as large as the effect.** Within-variant spread
  (A1→A2 = 188 ns) exceeds the A−B difference — single
  uninterleaved runs would have been inconclusive. Interleave
  and pair-difference any future comparisons.
- **First-run cold-start artifact.** A1's tail is anomalous
  (p99-max mean 16.8 µs, max 3.3 ms, stdev 3,321 ns vs ~800
  elsewhere). Discard or warm up the first run in future
  sessions. Excluding A1, deep tails are equal across variants.
- Residual condition difference: iiac-perf takes a systemd
  sleep-inhibit and runs a calibration pass before measuring;
  the example does neither. We think neither moves these
  numbers (inhibit only blocks suspend), but equalizing is
  cheap if a future comparison tightens further.

### Q2 iiac-perf fork deliberate

# no_std-first hardware tick-counter measurement probe.
# Consolidates four drifted copies (actor-x1, zc-ring-x1,
# iiac-perf, prospectively zc-msg-x1) into one crate; see
# notes/design.md for the founding rationale.
[package]
name = "tprobe"
version = "0.1.0-2"
edition = "2024"
rust-version = "1.97"
license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"
description = "no_std-first hardware tick-counter measurement probe"
publish = false

[features]
default = ["std"]
std = []

# Panicking sites are opt-in: each needs a site `#[allow]` +
# `// OK: …` justification (see AGENTS.md > Code Conventions).
[lints.clippy]
unwrap_used = "warn"
expect_used = "warn"

[dependencies]

# Used only by examples/tp_pc (analysis + presentation +
# pinning live in the example, not the crate — Q1/Q3 in
# notes/design.md are still open).
[dev-dependencies]
hdrhistogram = "7"
core_affinity = "0.8"

lines: # notes/design.md for the founding rationale.
[package]
name = "tprobe"
edition = "2024"
rust-version = "1.97"
license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: no_std tprobe core**

[design.md](design.md) settles tprobe's founding rationale and
the collection / analysis / presentation phase split (pluggable
Recorder, histogram-as-compression), but leaves open questions
that gate the crate's shape: which `no_std` histogram, the
recorder trait shape, the snapshot wire format, whether
iiac-perf's fork is deliberate, and where pinning /
`perf_event_open` live. Resolve the gating ones — recording
each decision in a chores design subsection — then seed the
`no_std` core from the existing copies under the new
constraints (no-alloc histogram, `&'static str` names,
cycle-counter trait, recorders, `std`-gated reporting).

- 0.1.0-0 prep: open cycle + chores design subsections for the
  three questions (done)
- 0.1.0-1 docs: no_std core phase split + unwrap lints (done)
- 0.1.0-2 feat: seed no_std core + tp_pc parity example (done)
  - ticks + ArrayRecorder + TProbe; `examples/tp_pc` is a
    parity port of iiac-perf's `tp-pc` (same loop, channels,
    band-table output)
- 0.1.0-3 run the tp_pc comparison vs iiac-perf's `tp-pc`;

#!/bin/bash
# A-B-B-A comparison: A = iiac-perf tp-pc (histogram at
# collection), B = tprobe examples/tp_pc (ArrayRecorder).
# 300 s each, cores 2,3; interleaved order cancels slow drift.
set -u
IIAC=/home/wink/data/prgs/rust/iiac-perf/target/release/iiac-perf
TP=/home/wink/data/prgs/rust/tprobe/target/release/examples/tp_pc

run_a() { echo "=== $1: iiac-perf tp-pc ($(date +%T)) ==="; "$IIAC" tp-pc --duration 300 --pin 2,3; }
run_b() { echo "=== $1: tprobe tp_pc ($(date +%T)) ==="; "$TP" --secs 300 --cores 2,3 --cap 50000000; }

run_a A1
run_b B1
run_b B2
run_a A2
echo "=== done ($(date +%T)) ==="

run_a A1

input.old_string:
- Residual condition difference: iiac-perf takes a systemd
  sleep-inhibit and runs a calibration pass before measuring;
  the example does neither. We think neither moves these
  numbers (inhibit only blocks suspend), but equalizing is

input.new_string:
- Residual condition difference: iiac-perf takes a systemd
  sleep-inhibit and runs a calibration pass before measuring;
  the example does neither. We think neither moves these
  numbers (inhibit only blocks suspend), but equalizing is
  cheap if a future comparison tightens further.

**Round 2** — after iiac-perf dropped `minstant` (calibration
now `std::time::Instant`, matching tprobe) and with a 60 s
discarded warmup ahead of A1. Raw output:
[tp-pc-abba-300s-r2-2026-07-12.txt](../tp-pc-abba-300s-r2-2026-07-12.txt).
Producer-loop summary:

| run | count | mean | mean min-p99 | p99-max mean |
|-----|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| A1  | 34,653,806 | 8,573 ns | 8,491 ns | 16,917 ns |
| B1  | 35,336,797 | 8,477 ns | 8,425 ns | 13,873 ns |
| B2  | 35,300,513 | 8,486 ns | 8,454 ns | 11,831 ns |
| A2  | 34,679,472 | 8,568 ns | 8,491 ns | 16,419 ns |

- **Warmup killed the drift.** Within-variant repeatability
  went from ~170–190 ns (round 1) to 5 ns (A) / 9 ns (B).
  Warmup + interleave is the protocol going forward.
- **Gap confirmed, minstant ruled out.** A−B = 96 / 82 ns
  (avg ~89 ns; round 1 ~102), throughput +1.9%, ~80 ns per
  record. Equalizing the calibration time source did not move
  the gap — expected, since tp-pc's measured loop reads
  `ticks::read_ticks()` directly and never touched minstant.
- **Why the percentage is small:** the mpsc round-trip is
  ~8.5 µs, so ~80–90 ns/record is ~1% of the iteration. The
  recorder-cost experiment (tight loop, no channel) will show
  the same absolute cost at tens of percent, and separates the
  open "large array streaming vs small cache-hot buffer"
  question.
- Unexplained tail wrinkle, recorded not attributed: both
  round-2 A runs show heavy p99-max bands (~16.5 µs mean)
  where round-1 A2 was clean (11.8 µs) and B1's max hit
  4.7 ms. We think the extreme tail is dominated by rare
  system events, not the variants.

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## feat: no_std tprobe core

Commits:

[design.md](../design.md) is the founding record; it leaves
three open questions [[1]] that set the crate's shape. This
cycle resolves them — recording each decision in a `###`
subsection below — then stands up the `no_std` core.

- Scope is the **probe** only — read a tick counter, record
  deltas into a named histogram, render a percentile band-table.
  The harness layer (loop sizing, overhead calibration, bench
  registry) stays in iiac-perf.
- Each open question below is a ladder step; the step lands the
  decision and this subsection records the outcome and its
  reasoning.

### Q1 no_std histogram crate vs hand-rolled

The core needs a `no_std`, no-alloc histogram to replace
`hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for embedded) — the real design
nut. We think a small fixed log-linear bucket table, sized like
the existing band scheme, is enough for the tail bands we report
(n5..n10 / p.999_999_999). Open: adopt an existing `no_std`
histogram crate, or hand-roll the fixed-bucket table. Evaluate
existing crates before hand-rolling; the choice drives the
core's shape. Data point from the tp_pc comparison (below):
hdrhistogram-at-collection measured ~87 ns per record vs a raw
array store — whatever wins Q1 should beat that by a wide
margin.

### tp_pc parity comparison: ArrayRecorder vs hdrhistogram

`examples/tp_pc` (tprobe `TProbe` + `ArrayRecorder`, raw-delta
store) vs iiac-perf's `tp-pc` (hdrhistogram record at
collection), identical loop / channels / band-table. Four
300 s runs, interleaved A-B-B-A (A = iiac-perf, B = tprobe) on
cores 2,3 to cancel slow drift; `--cap 50M` so the array never
fills. Raw output:
[tp-pc-abba-300s-2026-07-12.txt](../tp-pc-abba-300s-2026-07-12.txt).
Producer-loop summary:

| run | count | mean | mean min-p99 | p99-max mean |
|-----|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| A1  | 34,463,849 | 8,620 ns | 8,541 ns | 16,775 ns |
| B1  | 35,214,509 | 8,507 ns | 8,475 ns | 11,882 ns |
| B2  | 35,914,748 | 8,340 ns | 8,309 ns | 11,677 ns |
| A2  | 35,222,140 | 8,432 ns | 8,400 ns | 11,820 ns |

Findings:

- **ArrayRecorder measures ~1.2% lower means and ~2.1% higher
  throughput.** The drift-cancelling half-estimates agree —
  A−B mean 113 ns (A1−B1) and 92 ns (A2−B2), ~102 ns average;
  counts +0.75 M / +0.69 M per 300 s. We think the agreement
  across both pairs makes this a real effect, not drift.
- **~87 ns per record.** Round-trip period 8.611 µs (A) vs
  8.437 µs (B): 174 ns per iteration across the two records
  (one per thread). We think hdrhistogram's record path is
  the difference — larger than a naive bucket-increment
  estimate; the controlled recorder-cost experiment
  ([design.md](../design.md#first-experiment-recorder-cost))
  should confirm the mechanism before we lean on it.
- **The peer's recording cost lands inside the measured
  window.** We think that's why the *means* moved and not just
  throughput: in a ping-pong loop each thread's delta includes
  waiting for its peer, whose recording happens during that
  wait.
- **Drift is as large as the effect.** Within-variant spread
  (A1→A2 = 188 ns) exceeds the A−B difference — single
  uninterleaved runs would have been inconclusive. Interleave
  and pair-difference any future comparisons.
- **First-run cold-start artifact.** A1's tail is anomalous
  (p99-max mean 16.8 µs, max 3.3 ms, stdev 3,321 ns vs ~800
  elsewhere). Discard or warm up the first run in future
  sessions. Excluding A1, deep tails are equal across variants.
- Residual condition difference: iiac-perf takes a systemd
  sleep-inhibit and runs a calibration pass before measuring;
  the example does neither. We think neither moves these
  numbers (inhibit only blocks suspend), but equalizing is
  cheap if a future comparison tightens further.

### Q2 iiac-perf fork deliberate

iiac-perf today *forks* the probe rather than depending on it —
it uses `minstant` and its own band code. Open: is that fork
deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental. Deliberate →
consolidation is two-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg depend); incidental
→ three-repo (iiac-perf depends too). Confirm before assuming
which shape we are building for.

### Q3 pinning and perf_event_open placement

CPU pinning (`pin.rs`) and Linux `perf_event_open` counters sit
in different places across the copies — folded into the probe in
actor-x1, split into a separate `tp_runner` crate in zc-ring.
Open: put them behind this crate's `std` feature, or keep them
in a separate runner/harness crate as zc-ring split them. We
think the core stays `no_std` either way; this only decides
where the host-only machinery attaches.

# References

[1]: /notes/design.md#open-questions

hdrhistogram-at-collection measured ~87 ns per record vs a raw
array store — whatever wins Q1 should beat that by a wide
margin.

### tp_pc parity comparison: ArrayRecorder vs hdrhistogram

`examples/tp_pc` (tprobe `TProbe` + `ArrayRecorder`, raw-delta
store) vs iiac-perf's `tp-pc` (hdrhistogram record at
collection), identical loop / channels / band-table. Four
300 s runs, interleaved A-B-B-A (A = iiac-perf, B = tprobe) on
cores 2,3 to cancel slow drift; `--cap 50M` so the array never
fills. Raw output:
[tp-pc-abba-300s-2026-07-12.txt](../tp-pc-abba-300s-2026-07-12.txt).
Producer-loop summary:

| run | count | mean | mean min-p99 | p99-max mean |
|-----|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| A1  | 34,463,849 | 8,620 ns | 8,541 ns | 16,775 ns |
| B1  | 35,214,509 | 8,507 ns | 8,475 ns | 11,882 ns |
| B2  | 35,914,748 | 8,340 ns | 8,309 ns | 11,677 ns |
| A2  | 35,222,140 | 8,432 ns | 8,400 ns | 11,820 ns |

Findings:

- **ArrayRecorder measures ~1.2% lower means and ~2.1% higher
  throughput.** The drift-cancelling half-estimates agree —
  A−B mean 113 ns (A1−B1) and 92 ns (A2−B2), ~102 ns average;
  counts +0.75 M / +0.69 M per 300 s. We think the agreement
  across both pairs makes this a real effect, not drift.
- **~87 ns per record.** Round-trip period 8.611 µs (A) vs
  8.437 µs (B): 174 ns per iteration across the two records
  (one per thread). We think hdrhistogram's record path is
  the difference — larger than a naive bucket-increment
  estimate; the controlled recorder-cost experiment
  ([design.md](../design.md#first-experiment-recorder-cost))
  should confirm the mechanism before we lean on it.
- **The peer's recording cost lands inside the measured
  window.** We think that's why the *means* moved and not just
  throughput: in a ping-pong loop each thread's delta includes
  waiting for its peer, whose recording happens during that
  wait.
- **Drift is as large as the effect.** Within-variant spread
  (A1→A2 = 188 ns) exceeds the A−B difference — single
  uninterleaved runs would have been inconclusive. Interleave
  and pair-difference any future comparisons.
- **First-run cold-start artifact.** A1's tail is anomalous
  (p99-max mean 16.8 µs, max 3.3 ms, stdev 3,321 ns vs ~800
  elsewhere). Discard or warm up the first run in future
  sessions. Excluding A1, deep tails are equal across variants.
- Residual condition difference: iiac-perf takes a systemd
  sleep-inhibit and runs a calibration pass before measuring;
  the example does neither. We think neither moves these
  numbers (inhibit only blocks suspend), but equalizing is
  cheap if a future comparison tightens further.

**Round 2** — after iiac-perf dropped `minstant` (calibration
now `std::time::Instant`, matching tprobe) and with a 60 s
discarded warmup ahead of A1. Raw output:
[tp-pc-abba-300s-r2-2026-07-12.txt](../tp-pc-abba-300s-r2-2026-07-12.txt).
Producer-loop summary:

| run | count | mean | mean min-p99 | p99-max mean |
|-----|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| A1  | 34,653,806 | 8,573 ns | 8,491 ns | 16,917 ns |
| B1  | 35,336,797 | 8,477 ns | 8,425 ns | 13,873 ns |
| B2  | 35,300,513 | 8,486 ns | 8,454 ns | 11,831 ns |
| A2  | 34,679,472 | 8,568 ns | 8,491 ns | 16,419 ns |

- **Warmup killed the drift.** Within-variant repeatability
  went from ~170–190 ns (round 1) to 5 ns (A) / 9 ns (B).
  Warmup + interleave is the protocol going forward.
- **Gap confirmed, minstant ruled out.** A−B = 96 / 82 ns
  (avg ~89 ns; round 1 ~102), throughput +1.9%, ~80 ns per
  record. Equalizing the calibration time source did not move
  the gap — expected, since tp-pc's measured loop reads
  `ticks::read_ticks()` directly and never touched minstant.
- **Why the percentage is small:** the mpsc round-trip is
  ~8.5 µs, so ~80–90 ns/record is ~1% of the iteration. The
  recorder-cost experiment (tight loop, no channel) will show
  the same absolute cost at tens of percent, and separates the
  open "large array streaming vs small cache-hot buffer"
  question.
- Unexplained tail wrinkle, recorded not attributed: both
  round-2 A runs show heavy p99-max bands (~16.5 µs mean)
  where round-1 A2 was clean (11.8 µs) and B1's max hit
  4.7 ms. We think the extreme tail is dominated by rare
  system events, not the variants.

### Q2 iiac-perf fork deliberate

iiac-perf today *forks* the probe rather than depending on it —
it uses `minstant` and its own band code. Open: is that fork
deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental. Deliberate →
consolidation is two-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg depend); incidental
→ three-repo (iiac-perf depends too). Confirm before assuming
which shape we are building for.

### Q3 pinning and perf_event_open placement

CPU pinning (`pin.rs`) and Linux `perf_event_open` counters sit
in different places across the copies — folded into the probe in
actor-x1, split into a separate `tp_runner` crate in zc-ring.
Open: put them behind this crate's `std` feature, or keep them
in a separate runner/harness crate as zc-ring split them. We
think the core stays `no_std` either way; this only decides
where the host-only machinery attaches.

# References

[1]: /notes/design.md#open-questions

message.content: Which column are you using to look at the difference mean or mean min-p99, the difference in mean min-p99 was 0 for A1,A2 and where as B1,B2 was 29ns. Now 29ns of 8400+ is a small percentage but 0 difference is much much less, thoughts?

content.text:
I used the full `mean` column (plus counts) — worth being explicit about, because your observation exposes two things I glossed:

**Which column *should* carry the comparison?** Since we attributed the extreme tail to rare system events, the full mean imports exactly that noise into the A−B gap (A's heavy p99-max bands inflate it). On `mean min-p99` the gap is A1−B1 = 66 ns, A2−B2 = 37 ns — still B-faster in both pairs, but smaller and less pair-consistent than I quoted. The cleanest single metric is actually **count/throughput**: directly measured, no histogram, no trimming choice — +1.97%/+1.79% B in round 2, +2.2%/+2.0% in round 1. I'd lead with that, use trimmed mean second, and treat full mean as tail-contaminated.

**The 0 ns vs 29 ns repeatability is a real finding, not a rounding curiosity.** With ~31 M trimmed samples, the standard error of that mean is ~0.06 ns — so 29 ns between B1 and B2 is enormously significant statistically; it's systematic run-to-run *state* difference, not sampling noise. And A repeating to <1 ns (twice — consumer side too: 8,560/8,559) says the platform can be that stable. So something B does varies between runs that A doesn't. The obvious suspect, speculation-marked: **B allocates a fresh 400 MB buffer each run** — its address, transparent-huge-page assembly, and page-table layout differ run to run, while A's working set (~656 KB histogram) is compact and effectively identical every time. That's your "the large array matters" hypothesis showing up not as a mean shift but as *run-to-run variance* — and it argues the recorder-cost experiment should sweep array size (and ideally pin `--cap` to the actual need, ~36 M, rather than 50 M).

Let me fold this into the findings — it corrects my own summary in the direction your reading points:

input.old_string:
- **Warmup killed the drift.** Within-variant repeatability
  went from ~170–190 ns (round 1) to 5 ns (A) / 9 ns (B).
  Warmup + interleave is the protocol going forward.
- **Gap confirmed, minstant ruled out.** A−B = 96 / 82 ns
  (avg ~89 ns; round 1 ~102), throughput +1.9%, ~80 ns per
  record. Equalizing the calibration time source did not move
  the gap — expected, since tp-pc's measured loop reads
  `ticks::read_ticks()` directly and never touched minstant.
input.new_string:
- **Metric choice matters.** Full `mean` imports the extreme
  tail we attribute to system noise (A's heavy p99-max bands
  inflate its mean). Cleanest metric is **count/throughput**
  (directly measured, no trimming): B +1.97% / +1.79%.
  Second: `mean min-p99` (trimmed): A−B = 66 / 37 ns. Full
  mean (96 / 82 ns) overstates the gap.
- **Warmup killed the drift.** Within-variant repeatability
  on the trimmed mean went from ~170–190 ns (round 1) to
  **0 ns** (A: 8,491/8,491; consumer 8,560/8,559) and 29 ns
  (B). Warmup + interleave is the protocol going forward.
- **A repeats to <1 ns; B varies by 29 ns.** With ~31 M
  trimmed samples the standard error is ~0.06 ns, so B's
  29 ns run-to-run shift is systematic state, not sampling
  noise — and the platform demonstrably supports <1 ns
  repeatability (A). We think B's fresh 400 MB buffer per run
  (address, THP assembly, page-table layout differing run to
  run) is the leading suspect, vs A's compact ~656 KB
  histogram working set — the "large array" effect showing up
  as run-to-run variance rather than a mean shift. The
  recorder-cost experiment should sweep array size, and cap
  should be sized to need (~36 M), not padded (50 M).
- **Gap confirmed, minstant ruled out.** Equalizing the
  calibration time source did not move the gap — expected,
  since tp-pc's measured loop reads `ticks::read_ticks()`
  directly and never touched minstant. Per-record saving
  ~80 ns by full mean, less by trimmed mean; the controlled
  experiment owns the precise number.

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## feat: no_std tprobe core

Commits:

[design.md](../design.md) is the founding record; it leaves
three open questions [[1]] that set the crate's shape. This
cycle resolves them — recording each decision in a `###`
subsection below — then stands up the `no_std` core.

- Scope is the **probe** only — read a tick counter, record
  deltas into a named histogram, render a percentile band-table.
  The harness layer (loop sizing, overhead calibration, bench
  registry) stays in iiac-perf.
- Each open question below is a ladder step; the step lands the
  decision and this subsection records the outcome and its
  reasoning.

### Q1 no_std histogram crate vs hand-rolled

The core needs a `no_std`, no-alloc histogram to replace
`hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for embedded) — the real design
nut. We think a small fixed log-linear bucket table, sized like
the existing band scheme, is enough for the tail bands we report
(n5..n10 / p.999_999_999). Open: adopt an existing `no_std`
histogram crate, or hand-roll the fixed-bucket table. Evaluate
existing crates before hand-rolling; the choice drives the
core's shape. Data point from the tp_pc comparison (below):
hdrhistogram-at-collection measured ~80–90 ns per record vs a
raw array store, stable across two A-B-B-A rounds — whatever
wins Q1 should beat that by a wide margin.

| A1  | 34,463,849 | 8,620 ns | 8,541 ns | 16,775 ns |
| B1  | 35,214,509 | 8,507 ns | 8,475 ns | 11,882 ns |
| B2  | 35,914,748 | 8,340 ns | 8,309 ns | 11,677 ns |
| A2  | 35,222,140 | 8,432 ns | 8,400 ns | 11,820 ns |

  across both pairs makes this a real effect, not drift.
- **~87 ns per record.** Round-trip period 8.611 µs (A) vs

  (one per thread). We think hdrhistogram's record path is
  the difference — larger than a naive bucket-increment
  estimate; the controlled recorder-cost experiment

  should confirm the mechanism before we lean on it.
- **The peer's recording cost lands inside the measured
  window.** We think that's why the *means* moved and not just
  throughput: in a ping-pong loop each thread's delta includes
  waiting for its peer, whose recording happens during that
  wait.
- **Drift is as large as the effect.** Within-variant spread
  (A1→A2 = 188 ns) exceeds the A−B difference — single

now `std::time::Instant`, matching tprobe) and with a 60 s

|-----|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| A1  | 34,653,806 | 8,573 ns | 8,491 ns | 16,917 ns |
| B1  | 35,336,797 | 8,477 ns | 8,425 ns | 13,873 ns |
| B2  | 35,300,513 | 8,486 ns | 8,454 ns | 11,831 ns |
| A2  | 34,679,472 | 8,568 ns | 8,491 ns | 16,419 ns |

`examples/tp_pc` (tprobe `TProbe` + `ArrayRecorder`, raw-delta
store) vs iiac-perf's `tp-pc` (hdrhistogram record at
collection), identical loop / channels / band-table. Four
300 s runs, interleaved A-B-B-A (A = iiac-perf, B = tprobe) on
cores 2,3 to cancel slow drift; `--cap 50M` so the array never
fills. Raw output:
[tp-pc-abba-300s-2026-07-12.txt](../tp-pc-abba-300s-2026-07-12.txt).
Producer-loop summary:

| run | count | mean | mean min-p99 | p99-max mean |
|-----|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| A1  | 34,463,849 | 8,620 ns | 8,541 ns | 16,775 ns |
| B1  | 35,214,509 | 8,507 ns | 8,475 ns | 11,882 ns |
| B2  | 35,914,748 | 8,340 ns | 8,309 ns | 11,677 ns |
| A2  | 35,222,140 | 8,432 ns | 8,400 ns | 11,820 ns |

Findings:

- **ArrayRecorder measures ~1.2% lower means and ~2.1% higher
  throughput.** The drift-cancelling half-estimates agree —
  A−B mean 113 ns (A1−B1) and 92 ns (A2−B2), ~102 ns average;
  counts +0.75 M / +0.69 M per 300 s. We think the agreement
  across both pairs makes this a real effect, not drift.
- **~87 ns per record.** Round-trip period 8.611 µs (A) vs
  8.437 µs (B): 174 ns per iteration across the two records
  (one per thread). We think hdrhistogram's record path is
  the difference — larger than a naive bucket-increment
  estimate; the controlled recorder-cost experiment
  ([design.md](../design.md#first-experiment-recorder-cost))
  should confirm the mechanism before we lean on it.
- **The peer's recording cost lands inside the measured
  window.** We think that's why the *means* moved and not just
  throughput: in a ping-pong loop each thread's delta includes
  waiting for its peer, whose recording happens during that
  wait.
- **Drift is as large as the effect.** Within-variant spread
  (A1→A2 = 188 ns) exceeds the A−B difference — single
  uninterleaved runs would have been inconclusive. Interleave
  and pair-difference any future comparisons.
- **First-run cold-start artifact.** A1's tail is anomalous
  (p99-max mean 16.8 µs, max 3.3 ms, stdev 3,321 ns vs ~800
  elsewhere). Discard or warm up the first run in future
  sessions. Excluding A1, deep tails are equal across variants.
- Residual condition difference: iiac-perf takes a systemd
  sleep-inhibit and runs a calibration pass before measuring;
  the example does neither. We think neither moves these
  numbers (inhibit only blocks suspend), but equalizing is
  cheap if a future comparison tightens further.

**Round 2** — after iiac-perf dropped `minstant` (calibration
now `std::time::Instant`, matching tprobe) and with a 60 s
discarded warmup ahead of A1. Raw output:
[tp-pc-abba-300s-r2-2026-07-12.txt](../tp-pc-abba-300s-r2-2026-07-12.txt).
Producer-loop summary:

| run | count | mean | mean min-p99 | p99-max mean |
|-----|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| A1  | 34,653,806 | 8,573 ns | 8,491 ns | 16,917 ns |
| B1  | 35,336,797 | 8,477 ns | 8,425 ns | 13,873 ns |
| B2  | 35,300,513 | 8,486 ns | 8,454 ns | 11,831 ns |
| A2  | 34,679,472 | 8,568 ns | 8,491 ns | 16,419 ns |

- **Metric choice matters.** Full `mean` imports the extreme
  tail we attribute to system noise (A's heavy p99-max bands
  inflate its mean). Cleanest metric is **count/throughput**
  (directly measured, no trimming): B +1.97% / +1.79%.
  Second: `mean min-p99` (trimmed): A−B = 66 / 37 ns. Full
  mean (96 / 82 ns) overstates the gap.
- **Warmup killed the drift.** Within-variant repeatability
  on the trimmed mean went from ~170–190 ns (round 1) to
  **0 ns** (A: 8,491/8,491; consumer 8,560/8,559) and 29 ns
  (B). Warmup + interleave is the protocol going forward.
- **A repeats to <1 ns; B varies by 29 ns.** With ~31 M
  trimmed samples the standard error is ~0.06 ns, so B's
  29 ns run-to-run shift is systematic state, not sampling
  noise — and the platform demonstrably supports <1 ns
  repeatability (A). We think B's fresh 400 MB buffer per run
  (address, THP assembly, page-table layout differing run to
  run) is the leading suspect, vs A's compact ~656 KB
  histogram working set — the "large array" effect showing up
  as run-to-run variance rather than a mean shift. The
  recorder-cost experiment should sweep array size, and cap
  should be sized to need (~36 M), not padded (50 M).
- **Gap confirmed, minstant ruled out.** Equalizing the
  calibration time source did not move the gap — expected,
  since tp-pc's measured loop reads `ticks::read_ticks()`
  directly and never touched minstant. Per-record saving
  ~80 ns by full mean, less by trimmed mean; the controlled
  experiment owns the precise number.
- **Why the percentage is small:** the mpsc round-trip is
  ~8.5 µs, so ~80–90 ns/record is ~1% of the iteration. The
  recorder-cost experiment (tight loop, no channel) will show
  the same absolute cost at tens of percent, and separates the
  open "large array streaming vs small cache-hot buffer"
  question.
- Unexplained tail wrinkle, recorded not attributed: both
  round-2 A runs show heavy p99-max bands (~16.5 µs mean)
  where round-1 A2 was clean (11.8 µs) and B1's max hit
  4.7 ms. We think the extreme tail is dominated by rare
  system events, not the variants.

### Q2 iiac-perf fork deliberate

iiac-perf today *forks* the probe rather than depending on it —
it uses `minstant` and its own band code. Open: is that fork
deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental. Deliberate →
consolidation is two-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg depend); incidental
→ three-repo (iiac-perf depends too). Confirm before assuming
which shape we are building for.

### Q3 pinning and perf_event_open placement

CPU pinning (`pin.rs`) and Linux `perf_event_open` counters sit
in different places across the copies — folded into the probe in
actor-x1, split into a separate `tp_runner` crate in zc-ring.
Open: put them behind this crate's `std` feature, or keep them
in a separate runner/harness crate as zc-ring split them. We
think the core stays `no_std` either way; this only decides
where the host-only machinery attaches.

# References

[1]: /notes/design.md#open-questions

-raw array store, stable across two A-B-B-A rounds — whatever

message.content: Seems to me, the not unexpectedly, the warmup was important and we should discard run 1. And we should also do your suggested array changes. I also wonder if "many" shorter runs or fewer longer runs would be better. We could go for 1 600s run for each rather than 2 300, but maybe 10 60s runs would be better, thoughts?

Seems to me, not unexpectedly, the warmup was important and we should discard run 1. And we should also do your suggested array changes. I also wonder if "many" shorter runs or fewer longer runs would be better. We could go for 1 600s run for each rather than 2 300, but maybe 10 60s runs would be better, thoughts?

content.text:
My thoughts on run length, then I'll act on all three:

**Many shorter runs win for this comparison.** The key fact from round 2: within-run sampling error is ~0.06 ns, but *between-run* systematic state (B's 29 ns shift) is the actual noise floor. One 600 s run gives you a single draw of that run-level state — you literally cannot estimate your own uncertainty from it. Ten 60 s runs give ten draws: a distribution of per-run trimmed means, real confidence intervals on the A−B difference, principled outlier rejection (a run hit by a system event shows up and can be excluded), and better drift cancellation from tighter interleaving. Two caveats, both acceptable: per-run deep-tail resolution drops (~7 M samples/run resolves to ~p99.999 — fine for throughput/trimmed-mean comparison; deep-tail study belongs to the controlled recorder-cost experiment anyway), and thermal steady-state must fit in the run (it settles in seconds; 60 s is fine).

Bonus: 60 s runs make "size the cap to need" automatic — ~118 K/s × 60 s ≈ 7.1 M samples, so the default 8 M cap is the right size, no `--cap` flag at all. And if the THP/big-buffer hypothesis is right, B's repeatability should visibly tighten with the 8× smaller buffer — the run design doubles as a test of that hypothesis.

**Discarding round 1:** I'll demote it to a methodology note (kept as evidence for "warmup matters", excluded from findings numbers; raw file stays, labeled discarded — lab-notebook style). Restructuring now, then launching round 3: 60 s warmup + 10 alternating A/B pairs of 60 s (~21 min).

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## feat: no_std tprobe core

Commits:

[design.md](../design.md) is the founding record; it leaves
three open questions [[1]] that set the crate's shape. This
cycle resolves them — recording each decision in a `###`
subsection below — then stands up the `no_std` core.

- Scope is the **probe** only — read a tick counter, record
  deltas into a named histogram, render a percentile band-table.
  The harness layer (loop sizing, overhead calibration, bench
  registry) stays in iiac-perf.
- Each open question below is a ladder step; the step lands the
  decision and this subsection records the outcome and its
  reasoning.

### Q1 no_std histogram crate vs hand-rolled

The core needs a `no_std`, no-alloc histogram to replace
`hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for embedded) — the real design
nut. We think a small fixed log-linear bucket table, sized like
the existing band scheme, is enough for the tail bands we report
(n5..n10 / p.999_999_999). Open: adopt an existing `no_std`
histogram crate, or hand-roll the fixed-bucket table. Evaluate
existing crates before hand-rolling; the choice drives the
core's shape. Data point from the tp_pc comparison (below):
hdrhistogram-at-collection costs ~2% round-trip throughput vs
a raw array store, stable across two A-B-B-A rounds (~80 ns
per record by full mean, less by trimmed mean) — whatever wins
Q1 should beat that by a wide margin.

### Q2 iiac-perf fork deliberate

CPU pinning (`pin.rs`) and Linux `perf_event_open` counters sit
in different places across the copies — folded into the probe in
actor-x1, split into a separate `tp_runner` crate in zc-ring.

in a separate runner/harness crate as zc-ring split them. We
think the core stays `no_std` either way; this only decides
where the host-only machinery attaches.

#!/bin/bash
# Round 3: 10 alternating A/B pairs of 60 s each (A = iiac-perf
# tp-pc, B = tprobe examples/tp_pc), cores 2,3, after a 60 s
# discarded warmup. Short runs give 10 independent draws of the
# run-level state (real between-run variance, outlier detection);
# default 8M cap is sized to need at ~7.1M samples/60s.
set -u
IIAC=/home/wink/data/prgs/rust/iiac-perf/target/release/iiac-perf
TP=/home/wink/data/prgs/rust/tprobe/target/release/examples/tp_pc

echo "=== WARMUP (discard): iiac-perf tp-pc 60s ($(date +%T)) ==="
"$IIAC" tp-pc --duration 60 --pin 2,3 > /dev/null 2>&1

for i in $(seq 1 10); do
    echo "=== A$i: iiac-perf tp-pc ($(date +%T)) ==="
    "$IIAC" tp-pc --duration 60 --pin 2,3
    echo "=== B$i: tprobe tp_pc ($(date +%T)) ==="
    "$TP" --secs 60 --cores 2,3
done
echo "=== done ($(date +%T)) ==="

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## feat: no_std tprobe core

Commits:

[design.md](../design.md) is the founding record; it leaves
three open questions [[1]] that set the crate's shape. This
cycle resolves them — recording each decision in a `###`
subsection below — then stands up the `no_std` core.

- Scope is the **probe** only — read a tick counter, record
  deltas into a named histogram, render a percentile band-table.
  The harness layer (loop sizing, overhead calibration, bench
  registry) stays in iiac-perf.
- Each open question below is a ladder step; the step lands the
  decision and this subsection records the outcome and its
  reasoning.

### Q1 no_std histogram crate vs hand-rolled

The core needs a `no_std`, no-alloc histogram to replace
`hdrhistogram` (std, and heavy for embedded) — the real design
nut. We think a small fixed log-linear bucket table, sized like
the existing band scheme, is enough for the tail bands we report
(n5..n10 / p.999_999_999). Open: adopt an existing `no_std`
histogram crate, or hand-roll the fixed-bucket table. Evaluate
existing crates before hand-rolling; the choice drives the
core's shape. Data point from the tp_pc comparison (below):
hdrhistogram-at-collection costs ~2% round-trip throughput vs
a raw array store, stable across two A-B-B-A rounds (~80 ns
per record by full mean, less by trimmed mean) — whatever wins
Q1 should beat that by a wide margin.

### tp_pc parity comparison: ArrayRecorder vs hdrhistogram

`examples/tp_pc` (tprobe `TProbe` + `ArrayRecorder`, raw-delta
store) vs iiac-perf's `tp-pc` (hdrhistogram record at
collection), identical loop / channels / band-table, on cores
2,3, A = iiac-perf, B = tprobe.

**Round 1 (discarded — methodology lesson only).** Four 300 s
runs A-B-B-A with no warmup, `--cap 50M`. Raw output kept as
evidence:
[tp-pc-abba-300s-2026-07-12.txt](../tp-pc-abba-300s-2026-07-12.txt).
Discarded because within-variant drift (A1→A2 = 188 ns on the
trimmed mean, plus a grossly anomalous first-run tail: p99-max
mean 16.8 µs, max 3.3 ms) was as large as the A−B effect. Two
lessons kept: **warm up before measuring** (the first run of a
session is not usable) and **interleave + pair-difference**
(single uninterleaved runs would have been inconclusive). One
mechanism note that stands regardless: in a ping-pong loop each
thread's measured delta includes waiting for its peer, so we
think the peer's recording cost lands *inside* the measured
window — recording cost moves means, not just throughput.

**Round 2** — after iiac-perf dropped `minstant` (calibration
now `std::time::Instant`, matching tprobe) and with a 60 s
discarded warmup ahead of A1. Raw output:
[tp-pc-abba-300s-r2-2026-07-12.txt](../tp-pc-abba-300s-r2-2026-07-12.txt).
Producer-loop summary:

| run | count | mean | mean min-p99 | p99-max mean |
|-----|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| A1  | 34,653,806 | 8,573 ns | 8,491 ns | 16,917 ns |
| B1  | 35,336,797 | 8,477 ns | 8,425 ns | 13,873 ns |
| B2  | 35,300,513 | 8,486 ns | 8,454 ns | 11,831 ns |
| A2  | 34,679,472 | 8,568 ns | 8,491 ns | 16,419 ns |

- **Metric choice matters.** Full `mean` imports the extreme
  tail we attribute to system noise (A's heavy p99-max bands
  inflate its mean). Cleanest metric is **count/throughput**
  (directly measured, no trimming): B +1.97% / +1.79%.
  Second: `mean min-p99` (trimmed): A−B = 66 / 37 ns. Full
  mean (96 / 82 ns) overstates the gap.
- **Warmup killed the drift.** Within-variant repeatability
  on the trimmed mean went from ~170–190 ns (round 1) to
  **0 ns** (A: 8,491/8,491; consumer 8,560/8,559) and 29 ns
  (B). Warmup + interleave is the protocol going forward.
- **A repeats to <1 ns; B varies by 29 ns.** With ~31 M
  trimmed samples the standard error is ~0.06 ns, so B's
  29 ns run-to-run shift is systematic state, not sampling
  noise — and the platform demonstrably supports <1 ns
  repeatability (A). We think B's fresh 400 MB buffer per run
  (address, THP assembly, page-table layout differing run to
  run) is the leading suspect, vs A's compact ~656 KB
  histogram working set — the "large array" effect showing up
  as run-to-run variance rather than a mean shift. The
  recorder-cost experiment should sweep array size, and cap
  should be sized to need (~36 M), not padded (50 M).
- **Gap confirmed, minstant ruled out.** Equalizing the
  calibration time source did not move the gap — expected,
  since tp-pc's measured loop reads `ticks::read_ticks()`
  directly and never touched minstant. Per-record saving
  ~80 ns by full mean, less by trimmed mean; the controlled
  experiment owns the precise number.
- **Why the percentage is small:** the mpsc round-trip is
  ~8.5 µs, so ~80–90 ns/record is ~1% of the iteration. The
  recorder-cost experiment (tight loop, no channel) will show
  the same absolute cost at tens of percent, and separates the
  open "large array streaming vs small cache-hot buffer"
  question.
- Unexplained tail wrinkle, recorded not attributed: both
  round-2 A runs show heavy p99-max bands (~16.5 µs mean)
  where round-1 A2 was clean (11.8 µs) and B1's max hit
  4.7 ms. We think the extreme tail is dominated by rare
  system events, not the variants.

### Q2 iiac-perf fork deliberate

iiac-perf today *forks* the probe rather than depending on it —
it uses `minstant` and its own band code. Open: is that fork
deliberate (independent evolution) or incidental. Deliberate →
consolidation is two-repo (actor-x1 + zc-msg depend); incidental
→ three-repo (iiac-perf depends too). Confirm before assuming
which shape we are building for.

### Q3 pinning and perf_event_open placement

CPU pinning (`pin.rs`) and Linux `perf_event_open` counters sit
in different places across the copies — folded into the probe in
actor-x1, split into a separate `tp_runner` crate in zc-ring.
Open: put them behind this crate's `std` feature, or keep them
in a separate runner/harness crate as zc-ring split them. We
think the core stays `no_std` either way; this only decides
where the host-only machinery attaches.

# References

[1]: /notes/design.md#open-questions

The protocol lessons are baked in: discarded 60 s warmup always, and B's `--cap` computed from a ~140 K samples/s upper bound (sized to need, not padded — with the round-2 variance suspicion noted in the header).
- **`./tmp/`** — script `mkdir -p`s it and tees output to `tmp/tp-pc-cmp-<mode>-<secs>s-<timestamp>.txt`; `/tmp` added to `.gitignore` (jj respects it). Convention noted in the header + chores: a run worth keeping gets promoted from `./tmp/` into `notes/` as a lab record.
- Chores subsection now opens with a pointer to the driver.

Round 3 (still running, ~10 min left) was launched from the scratchpad script, but with identical parameters to `pairs` mode defaults — so it's reproducible via the saved driver going forward. These files join the pending `-3` commit; I'll fold the round-3 results in when the task completes and re-present the description.

message.content: One other note if 60s is better than 600s is 6s better yet?

content.text:
Somewhere between 600 s and 6 s the trade flips, and 6 s is plausibly past the flip. What pushes back as runs shrink:

- **Per-run transients become a bigger fraction.** Thread spawn, pinning, buffer alloc/prefault, first-iteration cache/branch warmup, and governor/P-state settling are roughly fixed costs per run. At 60 s they're noise; at 6 s the run may end before the machine reaches the steady state you're trying to compare. Below the settling time you're adding *bias*, not just variance — more runs can't average bias away.
- **Rare-event contamination per run grows 10×.** A single 1 ms scheduling hiccup shifts a 6 s run's mean ~10× more than a 60 s run's (~710 K vs ~7.1 M samples). Median-of-runs handles this, but only if most runs are clean — at 6 s a larger fraction of runs catches at least one event.
- **Per-run tail resolution drops** to ~p99.99 — fine for throughput/trimmed-mean, but the per-run tables get less individually meaningful.
- **Duty cycle falls** — fixed overheads (spawn, teardown, the warmup) eat a growing share of wall-clock, so "more runs per budget" partially cancels.

The statistics only favor shorter runs while per-run quality holds: the confidence on the A−B difference scales with √(number of runs) *if* run variance stays flat. If 6 s runs are individually noisier or biased, you lose more than you gain.

But this is exactly the kind of question we shouldn't theorize about when the driver makes it a one-liner: `scripts/tp-pc-cmp.sh pairs 6 20` (~3.5 min) vs the round-3 `pairs 60 10` data we're already collecting. Compare the normalized spread of per-run counts/trimmed means: if 6 s spread is comparable, shorter wins and we can even try 2–3 s; if it blows up, we've found the floor empirically. Worth doing as a follow-on after round 3 finishes (one benchmark at a time on those cores) — want me to queue it?

content.text:
Running (task `b8xmcgjk3`, ~5.5 min — warmup + 20 pairs of 6 s). First real exercise of the saved driver too: it's teeing into `./tmp/tp-pc-cmp-pairs-6s-<timestamp>.txt`.

When it lands I'll pull per-run counts and trimmed means for the 20 A and 20 B runs, and the key readout is the **within-variant scatter**: at 300 s, A repeated to <1 ns — if A's 6 s runs scatter by tens of ns, that's the transient/settling floor showing itself, and 60 s (rerun after this) stays the sweet spot; if they stay tight, shorter runs win and we can push further down.
